ability.definition: The skill or power to do something well.
ability.exampleSentence: She has the ability to speak three languages.
ability.contextPassage: We use 'ability' to talk about what a person can do. For example, a good swimmer has the ability to swim fast.

able.definition: Having the skill or power to do something.
able.exampleSentence: He was able to finish his homework before dinner.
able.contextPassage: We often say someone is 'able to' do something. It means they can do it without a big problem.

abroad.definition: In or to a country that is not your own country.
abroad.exampleSentence: My sister is going to study abroad next year.
abroad.contextPassage: People go abroad for work, travel, or school. Living abroad means you live in a different country.

accept.definition: To say yes to something that someone gives or offers you.
accept.exampleSentence: She accepted the gift from her friend with a smile.
accept.contextPassage: You can accept an invitation to a party or a job offer. When you accept something, you agree to take it.

accident.definition: A bad thing that happens by chance and that nobody planned.
accident.exampleSentence: He had a car accident on the way to work.
accident.contextPassage: Accidents can happen at home, on the road, or at school. We say something was an accident when nobody wanted it to happen.

according to.definition: As said by someone, or as shown in something.
according to.exampleSentence: According to the news, it will rain tomorrow.
according to.contextPassage: We use 'according to' before a person or source of information. For example, according to my teacher, the test is on Friday.

act.definition: Something that a person does; an action.
act.exampleSentence: Helping the old woman was a kind act.
act.contextPassage: An act is one thing that a person does. A kind act, like sharing food, can make someone very happy.

active.definition: Doing many things and moving around a lot.
active.exampleSentence: My grandfather is very active and walks every day.
active.contextPassage: An active person does not sit still for a long time. Playing sports or working in the garden are good ways to stay active.

actually.definition: Used to say what is really true, sometimes to show surprise.
actually.exampleSentence: I thought the film was short, but it was actually very long.
actually.contextPassage: We use 'actually' to show that the real situation is different. For example, you can say the food looks bad but it is actually delicious.

adult.definition: A person who is fully grown and is no longer a child.
adult.exampleSentence: Only adults can buy tickets for that event.
adult.contextPassage: In most countries, a person becomes an adult at the age of eighteen. Adults can make their own decisions and live alone.

advantage.definition: Something that helps you more than other people or things.
advantage.exampleSentence: Speaking English is a big advantage when you travel.
advantage.contextPassage: An advantage gives you a better chance to do well. For example, being tall is an advantage in basketball.

adventure.definition: An exciting or dangerous experience in a new place or situation.
adventure.exampleSentence: Their trip to the jungle was a real adventure.
adventure.contextPassage: People who like adventures enjoy trying new and exciting things. Hiking in the mountains or sailing the sea can be a great adventure.

advertise.definition: To tell many people about something, like a product or event, so they know about it.
advertise.exampleSentence: The shop will advertise its big sale on social media.
advertise.contextPassage: Companies advertise to get more customers. You can advertise a job, a product, or an event.

advertisement.definition: A message that tells people about a product or service so they will buy it.
advertisement.exampleSentence: I saw an advertisement for new shoes on TV last night.
advertisement.contextPassage: Advertisements appear on TV, online, and in magazines. A common short word for advertisement is 'ad'.

advertising.definition: The activity of telling many people about a product or service to get them to buy it.
advertising.exampleSentence: The company is advertising its new drink on the radio.
advertising.contextPassage: Advertising helps businesses find new customers. You will see advertising almost everywhere you go.

against.definition: Moving or going in the opposite direction to something.
against.exampleSentence: It was hard to walk against the strong wind today.
against.contextPassage: You can use 'against' to talk about direction or opposition. For example, swimming against a current is very difficult.

airline.definition: A company that uses planes to carry people or goods from place to place.
airline.exampleSentence: We booked our tickets with a cheap airline last week.
airline.contextPassage: An airline flies to many different cities and countries. You check in at the airport before your airline flight.

alive.exampleSentence: The small plant is still alive after the cold winter.
alive.contextPassage: You can use 'alive' to talk about people, animals, or plants. The opposite of alive is dead.

allow.definition: To say that someone can do something or have something.
allow.exampleSentence: The teacher will allow us to use a dictionary in the test.
allow.contextPassage: Parents allow or do not allow their children to do things. Rules at work or school also allow or stop certain actions.

almost.definition: Very close to something but not quite there or not exactly.
almost.exampleSentence: I almost missed the bus to school this morning.
almost.contextPassage: Use 'almost' when something is very close to being true. For example, the bag is almost full means a little space is left.

alone.definition: Without any other people; by yourself.
alone.exampleSentence: She sat alone at a table in the café.
alone.contextPassage: Being alone means no one else is with you. Some people enjoy time alone, but others feel lonely.

along.definition: Moving forward or going together with someone or something.
along.exampleSentence: Come along with us to the market this afternoon.
along.contextPassage: You can bring someone along when you go somewhere. 'Along' is often used with verbs like come, bring, or walk.

already.definition: Something that happened before now or before you expected it.
already.exampleSentence: I was surprised because she had already finished her homework.
already.contextPassage: Use 'already' to show that something happened sooner than expected. For example, the train already left means it left before you arrived.

although.definition: Used to introduce a fact that makes the other part of the sentence surprising.
although.exampleSentence: Although it was raining, we went to the park.
although.contextPassage: Use 'although' to connect two ideas that seem opposite. For example, although he was tired, he kept working.

among.definition: In a group of people or things, mixed together with them.
among.exampleSentence: She found her keys among the books on the table.
among.contextPassage: You can use 'among' when something is inside a group. For example, one student among thirty classmates.

amount.definition: How much of something there is in total.
amount.exampleSentence: I was surprised by the large amount of food on the table.
amount.contextPassage: Use 'amount' for things you cannot count, like water or money. For example, a small amount of sugar.

ancient.definition: A person who is very, very old.
ancient.exampleSentence: The ancient sat quietly in the corner of the room.
ancient.contextPassage: This word is not very common as a noun. People more often use it as an adjective to describe old things.

ankle.definition: The joint between your foot and your leg.
ankle.exampleSentence: She hurt her ankle when she fell on the stairs.
ankle.contextPassage: The ankle helps you walk and move your foot up and down. You can twist your ankle if you step the wrong way.

any more.definition: Used to say something has stopped or no longer happens.
any more.exampleSentence: He doesn't play football any more.
any more.contextPassage: Use 'any more' in negative sentences to show a change. For example, she doesn't live here any more.

anyway.definition: Used to say something is true or happens even so.
anyway.exampleSentence: It was raining, but we went to the park anyway.
anyway.contextPassage: 'Anyway' can also go back to a topic in a conversation. For example, anyway, let me tell you what happened.

anywhere.definition: In, at, or to any place.
anywhere.exampleSentence: I can't find my phone anywhere in the house.
anywhere.contextPassage: Use 'anywhere' in questions and negative sentences. For example, did you go anywhere last weekend?

app.definition: A small program you use on a phone or tablet.
app.exampleSentence: I downloaded a new app to learn Spanish on my phone.
app.contextPassage: Apps can help you with many things like maps, music, or shopping. You can find free apps in an app store.

appear.definition: To become visible or come into view.
appear.exampleSentence: A cat suddenly appeared from behind the wall.
appear.contextPassage: 'Appear' means something or someone comes so you can see them. For example, the sun appeared after the rain stopped.

argue.definition: To disagree with someone and talk in an angry way.
argue.exampleSentence: The two brothers argued about the TV remote.
argue.contextPassage: When people argue, they each say why they think they are right. You can argue with a friend or a family member.

argument.definition: An angry talk between people who do not agree.
argument.exampleSentence: They had a big argument about money last night.
argument.contextPassage: An argument happens when two or more people strongly disagree. After an argument, people sometimes feel sad or angry.

army.definition: A large group of soldiers who fight on land.
army.exampleSentence: The army moved into the town early in the morning.
army.contextPassage: A country's army protects the country on the ground. Soldiers train hard before they join the army.

arrange.definition: To plan or organize something so it is ready or in order.
arrange.exampleSentence: She will arrange the chairs in a circle for the meeting.
arrange.contextPassage: You can arrange a party or a trip before it happens. People also arrange flowers in a vase.

asleep.definition: In the state of sleeping; not awake.
asleep.exampleSentence: The baby was asleep in her room by eight o'clock.
asleep.contextPassage: You fall asleep when you stop being awake. People are often asleep at night.

assistant.definition: A person who helps someone else with their work or tasks.
assistant.exampleSentence: The assistant helped the doctor with the patient.
assistant.contextPassage: An assistant works with another person to make their job easier. You can find an assistant in offices, shops, and hospitals.

attack.definition: A sudden act of violence against a person or place.
attack.exampleSentence: The dog made a sudden attack on the smaller animal.
attack.contextPassage: An attack can happen in a war or in the street. People try to stay safe and prevent attacks.

attention.definition: When you focus your mind on something and listen or watch carefully.
attention.exampleSentence: The teacher asked the students to pay attention to the board.
attention.contextPassage: You give your attention when you listen carefully to someone. It is hard to pay attention when there is a lot of noise.

attractive.definition: Nice to look at or pleasing in some way.
attractive.exampleSentence: The shop window looked very attractive with its colorful display.
attractive.contextPassage: People often say a place or a person is attractive when they like what they see. Bright colors and good design can make something attractive.

audience.definition: A group of people who watch or listen to a show, speech, or performance.
audience.exampleSentence: The audience clapped loudly at the end of the concert.
audience.contextPassage: An audience can be big or small. People in the audience sit together and watch the same thing.

author.definition: A person who writes a book or story.
author.exampleSentence: My favorite author wrote a new book last year.
author.contextPassage: An author spends a lot of time writing stories or books. Some authors write for children and some write for adults.

available.definition: Ready to use or possible to get at this time.
available.exampleSentence: The doctor is available to see you on Monday afternoon.
available.contextPassage: Something available is not busy or taken. You can ask if a room or a person is available before you make a plan.

average.definition: A number you get by adding amounts together and dividing by how many there are.
average.exampleSentence: The average score in the test was seventy out of one hundred.
average.contextPassage: You can find the average of a group of numbers. Teachers often calculate the average score of their students.

avoid.definition: To stay away from someone or something on purpose.
avoid.exampleSentence: She tried to avoid eating too much sugar every day.
avoid.contextPassage: People avoid things that are dangerous or bad for them. You can also avoid a person if you do not want to see them.

award.definition: A prize or honor given to someone for doing something well.
award.exampleSentence: She received an award for being the best student this year.
award.contextPassage: Schools and companies often give awards to people who work hard. Winning an award makes most people feel very proud.

awful.exampleSentence: The weather was awful during our trip last week.
awful.contextPassage: You can use awful to describe food, weather, or a bad experience. People often say 'That's awful!' when they hear bad news.

background.definition: The family, place, and experiences from a person's past life.
background.exampleSentence: She has a background in cooking from her home country.
background.contextPassage: You can ask someone about their background to learn where they grew up. People from different backgrounds have different traditions and habits.

badly.definition: In a sick or unwell way; not feeling good in the body.
badly.exampleSentence: He looked badly after spending three days in bed.
badly.contextPassage: You can say someone looks or feels badly when they are not well. This word describes how a person feels when they are sick.

bar.definition: A long, hard, straight piece of metal or wood.
bar.exampleSentence: He used an iron bar to open the old gate.
bar.contextPassage: A bar can be made of metal, wood, or another hard material. It is usually much longer than it is wide.

baseball.definition: A sport where players hit a ball with a bat and run around four bases to score points.
baseball.exampleSentence: We watched a baseball game at the stadium on Saturday.
baseball.contextPassage: Baseball is very popular in the United States and Japan. Two teams of nine players each take turns hitting and fielding.

based.definition: To use something as the starting point or main idea for something else.
based.exampleSentence: The movie was based on a true story from the 1980s.
based.contextPassage: You often say a book or film is based on something real or on another story. It means the main idea came from that thing.

basketball.definition: A sport where two teams of five players try to throw a ball through a hoop to score points.
basketball.exampleSentence: My brother plays basketball with his friends every Saturday.
basketball.contextPassage: In basketball, the team with the most points at the end wins the game. Players dribble, pass, and shoot the ball to score.

bean.definition: A small seed that grows in a long pod and is used as food.
bean.exampleSentence: She added some beans to the soup for dinner tonight.
bean.contextPassage: Beans are a healthy food that many people eat every day. You can buy them fresh, dried, or in a can at the shop.

bear.definition: A large, heavy wild animal with thick fur and a short tail.
bear.exampleSentence: We saw a big brown bear near the river in the forest.
bear.contextPassage: Bears eat both plants and animals because they are omnivores. They usually sleep through the cold winter months.

beat.definition: A single hit or knock that makes a sound.
beat.exampleSentence: I heard one loud beat on the drum before the music started.
beat.contextPassage: A beat is the sound you hear when something hits another thing once. Drums make a clear beat when someone hits them.

beef.definition: The meat that comes from a cow or bull, used as food.
beef.exampleSentence: My dad grilled some beef for dinner last Sunday evening.
beef.contextPassage: Beef is a common type of meat in many countries around the world. You can cook it in many ways, like frying, boiling, or grilling.

behave.definition: To act in a good and polite way toward other people.
behave.exampleSentence: The children will behave well at the school event tomorrow.
behave.contextPassage: Parents often tell their children to behave when they are in a public place. It means you should be polite and follow the rules.

belong.definition: To be in the right place for something.
belong.exampleSentence: This book belongs on the shelf in my room.
belong.contextPassage: You can use 'belong' to say where something should go. For example, plates belong in the kitchen.

belt.definition: A long, thin piece of cloth or leather that you wear around your waist.
belt.exampleSentence: He wore a brown belt with his blue jeans.
belt.contextPassage: People wear a belt to keep their trousers up. You can also wear a belt to look nice.

best.definition: The greatest effort that a person is able to give.
best.exampleSentence: She did her best on the school test.
best.contextPassage: You can say 'do your best' when you try as hard as you can. It means you gave all your effort.

better.definition: A person who is more skilled or stronger than another person.
better.exampleSentence: He met his better at the chess competition.
better.contextPassage: You can use 'better' to talk about someone who is more able than you. It is not a very common use of this word.

between.definition: A short, thick needle with a small eye, used for sewing heavy cloth.
between.exampleSentence: She used a between to sew the thick fabric together.
between.contextPassage: A between is a type of sewing needle. People use it when the cloth is very thick or hard to push through.

billion number.definition: The number one thousand million, written as 1,000,000,000.
billion number.exampleSentence: There are about eight billion people in the world.
billion number.contextPassage: We use 'billion' for very large numbers. You often see it in news about money or world population.

bin.definition: A large container used to keep or store things.
bin.exampleSentence: She put the old papers in the bin.
bin.contextPassage: People use a bin to throw away rubbish. You can also use a bin to store food or other items.

biology.definition: The school subject or science about living things like animals and plants.
biology.exampleSentence: We learned about plants in our biology class today.
biology.contextPassage: Biology is a science subject at school. Students study animals, plants, and the human body in biology.

birth.definition: The time when a baby comes out and starts its life.
birth.exampleSentence: The birth of her baby happened in the hospital.
birth.contextPassage: We use 'birth' to talk about when a person or animal is born. Your date of birth is the day you were born.

biscuit.definition: A small, flat, baked food that is sweet and usually hard or crisp.
biscuit.exampleSentence: She had a biscuit and a cup of tea.
biscuit.contextPassage: People eat biscuits as a snack. You can dip a biscuit in your tea or coffee.

bit.definition: A small piece of metal that goes in a horse's mouth to help control it.
bit.exampleSentence: The rider put the bit in the horse's mouth.
bit.contextPassage: A bit is part of a horse's equipment. The rider holds the reins, which are connected to the bit.

blank.definition: An empty space on a page where you need to write something.
blank.exampleSentence: Please write your name in the blank on the form.
blank.contextPassage: You will see blanks on tests and forms. You need to fill in the blank with the correct word or information.

blood.definition: A member of a street gang called the Bloods in Los Angeles.
blood.exampleSentence: The police said he was a Blood from Los Angeles.
blood.contextPassage: The Bloods is a gang from Los Angeles, USA. Members of this gang are called Bloods.

blow.definition: A strong movement of wind or air.
blow.exampleSentence: A strong blow knocked the umbrella out of my hand.
blow.contextPassage: A blow can happen during a storm or bad weather. It can move small objects and make it hard to walk outside.

board.definition: A flat, thin, and long piece of wood or similar material.
board.exampleSentence: He nailed a board to the wall to fix the hole.
board.contextPassage: People use boards to build houses, floors, and furniture. A board is usually flat and hard.

boil.definition: A painful red lump on the skin filled with a yellow liquid.
boil.exampleSentence: She had a boil on her neck and it really hurt.
boil.contextPassage: A boil is caused by an infection under the skin. A doctor can help you treat a bad boil.

bone.definition: A hard white part inside the body of a person or animal.
bone.exampleSentence: He broke a bone in his hand during the game.
bone.contextPassage: Bones give your body its shape and help you move. Drinking milk is good for strong bones.

borrow.definition: The curve or slope on a surface that makes a ball move sideways.
borrow.exampleSentence: He did not see the borrow on the green and missed the hole.
borrow.contextPassage: In golf, a borrow is the slope on the grass near the hole. Players check the borrow before they hit the ball.

boss.definition: A person who is in charge of other people at work.
boss.exampleSentence: My boss told me to finish the report by Friday.
boss.contextPassage: A boss tells workers what to do and checks their work. In a big company, there can be many bosses.

bottom.exampleSentence: She found her keys at the bottom of her bag.
bottom.contextPassage: The bottom is the part that is furthest down. You can talk about the bottom of a box, a hill, or a page.

bowl.definition: A round, open container used to hold food or other things.
bowl.exampleSentence: She put the soup into a big bowl on the table.
bowl.contextPassage: People use bowls for food like soup, salad, and cereal. A bowl is usually wider at the top than at the bottom.

brain.definition: The part inside your head that controls your body and your thoughts.
brain.exampleSentence: The doctor said the brain needs rest after an injury.
brain.contextPassage: Your brain helps you think, remember, and feel things. It also controls how your body moves.

bridge.definition: A structure built over a river or road so people can cross it.
bridge.exampleSentence: We walked across the old bridge over the river.
bridge.contextPassage: A bridge connects two places separated by water or a road. Some bridges are very long and carry cars and trains.

bright.definition: A flat brush with a short head used by artists to paint with oil or acrylic.
bright.exampleSentence: She used a bright to paint thick lines on the canvas.
bright.contextPassage: A bright is a type of brush used in painting. Artists choose a bright when they want short, strong brush strokes.

cooker.definition: A machine in the kitchen that you use to heat and cook food.
cooker.exampleSentence: She put the soup on the cooker to heat it up.
cooker.contextPassage: A cooker can use gas or electricity to cook food. Most families use a cooker every day to make meals.

copy.definition: Something that looks exactly the same as another thing.
copy.exampleSentence: She made a copy of the letter for her records.
copy.contextPassage: You can make a copy of a document at school or at work. A copy looks the same as the original thing.

corner.definition: The place where two lines or two walls meet.
corner.exampleSentence: He put the lamp in the corner of the room.
corner.contextPassage: A corner is where two streets or two walls meet. You can find a corner inside a room or outside on a street.

correctly.definition: In a way that is right and without mistakes.
correctly.exampleSentence: She answered all the questions correctly on the test.
correctly.contextPassage: When you do something correctly, you do it the right way. Your teacher will tell you if you answered correctly.

count.definition: The act of adding up a number of things to find the total.
count.exampleSentence: The teacher did a count of all the students in the class.
count.contextPassage: A count helps you find out how many things there are. You can do a count of people, items, or anything else.

couple.definition: Two people who are in a romantic relationship together.
couple.exampleSentence: The couple walked hand in hand in the park.
couple.contextPassage: A couple often spends a lot of time together. They go on dates and do activities together.

cover.definition: A flat piece that goes on top of a pot or box to close it.
cover.exampleSentence: She put the cover on the pot to keep the food warm.
cover.contextPassage: A cover keeps food warm and stops dirt from getting in. You can find a cover on a pot, a box, or a jar.

crazy.definition: A person who behaves in a very strange or unusual way.
crazy.exampleSentence: People on the street thought he was a crazy.
crazy.contextPassage: Someone called a crazy acts in ways that seem very different from normal. This word is informal and some people find it unkind.

creative.definition: A person whose job is to make new and original ideas, for example in advertising.
creative.exampleSentence: The agency hired a creative to design the new advertisement.
creative.contextPassage: A creative thinks of new ideas for posters, videos, or other media. They work with a team to make interesting content.

crime.definition: Something that a person does that breaks the law.
crime.exampleSentence: Stealing money from a shop is a crime.
crime.contextPassage: When someone does a crime, the police can arrest them. A crime can be small, like stealing, or very serious.

criminal.definition: A person who has done something that breaks the law.
criminal.exampleSentence: The police caught the criminal near the bank.
criminal.contextPassage: A criminal is someone who did a crime. They can go to court and may have to go to prison.

cross.definition: A shape made by one straight line going over another straight line.
cross.exampleSentence: She drew a cross on the paper to mark the answer.
cross.contextPassage: A cross looks like a plus sign or the letter X. People use a cross to mark something or as a symbol.

crowd.definition: A large group of people in the same place at the same time.
crowd.exampleSentence: A big crowd waited outside the stadium before the game.
crowd.contextPassage: You often see a crowd at concerts or markets. The word crowd means many people together in one place.

crowded.definition: To move forward by pushing other people out of the way.
crowded.exampleSentence: People crowded toward the exit at the end of the show.
crowded.contextPassage: When everyone wants to leave at the same time, people crowd the doors. You can also say people crowded around something they wanted to see.

cry.definition: The act of letting tears fall from your eyes because you feel sad or upset.
cry.exampleSentence: She had a long cry after she heard the bad news.
cry.contextPassage: A cry can happen when you feel very sad or hurt. Sometimes people have a good cry and feel better after.

cupboard.definition: A piece of furniture with doors and shelves for storing food or dishes.
cupboard.exampleSentence: She put the clean plates back in the cupboard after dinner.
cupboard.contextPassage: A cupboard is common in a kitchen or bedroom. You can open a cupboard to find cups, food, or other things.

curly.definition: A person or animal that has hair which grows in round, spiral shapes.
curly.exampleSentence: The little curly ran into the room with her hair bouncing.
curly.contextPassage: People sometimes call a child or pet with curly hair a curly. It is a friendly and informal word.

cycle.definition: A set of events that happen again and again in the same order.
cycle.exampleSentence: The cycle of the seasons goes from spring to winter and back again.
cycle.contextPassage: A cycle always ends and then starts again from the beginning. The water cycle and the sleep cycle are common examples.

daily.definition: Something that happens or is done every single day.
daily.exampleSentence: Reading the news is a daily habit for many people.
daily.contextPassage: A daily routine includes things like eating breakfast and brushing your teeth. If something is daily, you do it or see it every day.

danger.definition: A situation where something bad or harmful could happen to you.
danger.exampleSentence: The sign warned drivers about the danger on the icy road.
danger.contextPassage: You are in danger when something around you could hurt you. It is important to stay away from danger and stay safe.

data.definition: Information or numbers that you collect to learn about something.
data.exampleSentence: The teacher looked at the test data to see how students did.
data.contextPassage: Data can be numbers, answers, or facts collected from a study or survey. Computers store and read large amounts of data very quickly.

dead.definition: The people who have already died and are no longer alive.
dead.exampleSentence: The town built a monument to honor the dead from the old war.
dead.contextPassage: We often use the dead to talk about people who lived before us. People may visit cemeteries to remember the dead.

deal.definition: A share or part of something that is given or divided between people.
deal.exampleSentence: Each child got a fair deal of the candy from the bag.
deal.contextPassage: A deal is a portion of something shared between two or more people. You can also use deal to talk about an agreement between people.

dear.definition: A word you say to show surprise, worry, or a little shock about something.
dear.exampleSentence: Oh dear, I forgot my keys inside the house again!
dear.contextPassage: People say dear when something small goes wrong or is surprising. It is a very common and gentle way to show you are worried or surprised.

dear exclam..definition: A word you say when you are surprised or a little worried about something.
dear exclam..exampleSentence: Oh dear, I forgot my keys at home again.
dear exclam..contextPassage: People say 'oh dear' when something small goes wrong. It shows they are surprised or sad about it.

death.definition: The end of a person's or animal's life; when something stops living.
death.exampleSentence: The death of her cat made her very sad.
death.contextPassage: We talk about death when a person or animal stops living. It is the opposite of birth.

decision.definition: A choice you make after thinking about something.
decision.exampleSentence: She made a decision to move to a new city.
decision.contextPassage: You make a decision when you choose between two or more things. It can be a big or small choice.

deep.definition: The deep part of the ocean or a lake, far below the surface.
deep.exampleSentence: Strange fish live in the deep of the ocean.
deep.contextPassage: We use the deep to talk about the lowest, darkest part of the sea. Very few people have seen the deep.

definitely.exampleSentence: She will definitely come to the party tonight.
definitely.contextPassage: You say definitely when you are completely sure about something. It is stronger than saying probably or maybe.

degree.definition: A certificate you get from a university after you finish your studies.
degree.exampleSentence: He got a degree in business from the university.
degree.contextPassage: Students study for several years to get a degree. A degree shows that you finished a course at university.

dentist.definition: A doctor who takes care of your teeth.
dentist.exampleSentence: I went to the dentist because my tooth hurt.
dentist.contextPassage: A dentist checks your teeth and fixes problems. You should visit a dentist at least once a year.

department.definition: One part or section of a large place like a store or company.
department.exampleSentence: She works in the sales department at her office.
department.contextPassage: Big stores and companies have many departments. Each department does a different job or sells different things.

depend.definition: To need something or someone to be true or to happen first.
depend.exampleSentence: The price will depend on the size you choose.
depend.contextPassage: We use depend on to say that one thing changes because of another. For example, your plans can depend on the weather.

desert.definition: A punishment or reward that someone truly earns by what they did.
desert.exampleSentence: The bad man got his just deserts in the end.
desert.contextPassage: We use deserts to say someone got what they deserved. It is usually used in the phrase just deserts.

designer.definition: A person whose job is to plan how something will look or work.
designer.exampleSentence: The designer made a beautiful cover for the new book.
designer.contextPassage: A designer can work on clothes, buildings, or websites. They plan the look of things before they are made.

destroy.definition: To damage something so badly that it cannot be used or fixed.
destroy.exampleSentence: The big fire destroyed several houses on our street.
destroy.contextPassage: When something is destroyed, it is completely broken or gone. A storm can destroy buildings and trees.

detective.definition: A police officer who tries to find out who did a crime.
detective.exampleSentence: The detective found important clues at the scene.
detective.contextPassage: A detective looks for evidence and talks to people to solve a crime. You can also see detectives as characters in films and books.

develop.definition: To grow or get better over time.
develop.exampleSentence: She will develop her cooking skills at the new class.
develop.contextPassage: People develop new skills when they practice a lot. A city can also develop and get bigger over the years.

device.definition: A small machine or tool made to do a specific job.
device.exampleSentence: He used a device to measure the temperature outside.
device.contextPassage: A phone is a device you use to call and message people. Many devices need batteries or electricity to work.

diary.definition: A book where you write about things that happen each day.
diary.exampleSentence: She wrote about her trip in her diary last night.
diary.contextPassage: Some people write in their diary every evening before bed. A diary is a good way to remember important moments.

differently.definition: In a way that is not the same as before or as others.
differently.exampleSentence: He answered the question differently from his classmates.
differently.contextPassage: You can try to do something differently if it does not work. Two people can look at the same thing differently.

digital.definition: A digital version of something that exists online or on a screen.
digital.exampleSentence: I bought the digital version of the book online.
digital.contextPassage: A digital copy is easy to store on your phone or computer. Many newspapers now have a digital option you can read anywhere.

direct.definition: To tell people what to do and where to go to reach a goal.
direct.exampleSentence: The manager will direct the team during the big project.
direct.contextPassage: A teacher can direct students to the right answer with questions. It is important to direct people clearly so they are not confused.

direction.definition: The way you need to go to reach a place.
direction.exampleSentence: She walked in the wrong direction and got lost.
direction.contextPassage: You can use a map to find the right direction. North, south, east, and west are names for different directions.

director.definition: A person who is in charge of a project, company, or film.
director.exampleSentence: The director told the actors where to stand on stage.
director.contextPassage: A film director makes decisions about how a movie will look. A director at a company is responsible for an important part of the work.

disagree.definition: To have a different opinion from someone else.
disagree.exampleSentence: My friend and I disagree about which film is the best.
disagree.contextPassage: It is normal to disagree with people sometimes. You can disagree with someone and still be polite and friendly.

disappear.definition: To stop being visible or to go away suddenly.
disappear.exampleSentence: The cat disappeared behind the big bush in the garden.
disappear.contextPassage: The sun will disappear behind the clouds when it gets cloudy. Things can disappear quickly if you are not careful with them.

disaster.definition: A very bad event that causes a lot of damage or harm to people.
disaster.exampleSentence: The big storm was a disaster for the small town.
disaster.contextPassage: A flood or earthquake can be a natural disaster. After a disaster, people often help each other to rebuild their homes.

discover.definition: To find or learn about something for the first time.
discover.exampleSentence: She was happy to discover a new café near her house.
discover.contextPassage: Children love to discover new things when they explore outside. Scientists discover new plants and animals every year.

discovery.definition: Something new that someone finds or learns for the first time.
discovery.exampleSentence: The discovery of gold in the river was very exciting.
discovery.contextPassage: Scientists make discoveries when they find something nobody knew before. A discovery can change the way people think about the world.

discussion.definition: A talk between two or more people about a topic.
discussion.exampleSentence: We had a discussion about our plans for the weekend.
discussion.contextPassage: A discussion is when people share their ideas and listen to others. Teachers often ask students to have a discussion in class.

disease.definition: A serious illness that makes a person, animal, or plant very sick.
disease.exampleSentence: The doctor said he had a disease and needed medicine.
disease.contextPassage: A disease can spread from one person to another. Some diseases are very dangerous, but doctors can treat many of them.

distance.definition: The amount of space between two places or things.
distance.exampleSentence: The distance between the two towns is about 50 kilometres.
distance.contextPassage: You can measure distance in kilometres or miles. A long distance means two places are very far apart.

divorced.definition: To legally end a marriage so two people are no longer husband and wife.
divorced.exampleSentence: Her parents divorced when she was ten years old.
divorced.contextPassage: When two people divorce, they stop being married. After they divorced, they both lived in different houses.

document.definition: An official paper or file that shows important information.
document.exampleSentence: She needed to sign the document before she could travel.
document.contextPassage: A passport is an important travel document. You should keep your documents in a safe place.

double.definition: Two times the amount or size of something.
double.exampleSentence: He asked for a double portion of rice at the restaurant.
double.contextPassage: Double means you have two times more of something. If the price is double, you pay two times the normal amount.

download.definition: A file that you copy from the internet to your computer or phone.
download.exampleSentence: The download of the new app only took a few seconds.
download.contextPassage: You can find free downloads on many websites. After the download is finished, you can open the file on your device.

downstairs.definition: The lower floor of a building, closer to the ground.
downstairs.exampleSentence: The kitchen and living room are downstairs in our house.
downstairs.contextPassage: Downstairs is the part of the house on the ground floor. She went downstairs to get a glass of water.

drama.definition: A story written for actors to perform on stage or on TV.
drama.exampleSentence: We watched a drama about a family living in a small town.
drama.contextPassage: A drama tells a story through the words and actions of the actors. Many people enjoy watching dramas on television in the evening.

drawing.definition: A picture made by moving a pen or pencil on paper.
drawing.exampleSentence: She made a drawing of her dog in art class.
drawing.contextPassage: A drawing can show people, places, or things. You can make a drawing with a pencil, pen, or coloured markers.

dream.definition: Pictures and events that you see in your mind while you are sleeping.
dream.exampleSentence: He had a dream about flying over a big city.
dream.contextPassage: Most people have dreams every night when they sleep. Some dreams feel very real, but they are not really happening.

drive.definition: A strong feeling inside you that makes you want to work hard and reach your goals.
drive.exampleSentence: She has a lot of drive and never gives up easily.
drive.contextPassage: We use drive to describe someone very motivated and ambitious. You might say a good worker has real drive.

driving.definition: To push or move something forward by using force or power.
driving.exampleSentence: The strong wind is driving the leaves across the street.
driving.contextPassage: We use driving when something causes movement or change. For example, wind can drive rain against a window.

drop.definition: A very small, round piece of liquid that falls from something.
drop.exampleSentence: A drop of water fell from the tap onto the floor.
drop.contextPassage: We often talk about drops of rain or drops of medicine. A drop is very small, just one tiny bit of liquid.

drug.definition: A substance that a doctor gives you to help your body feel better or fight illness.
drug.exampleSentence: The doctor gave her a drug to help with the pain.
drug.contextPassage: Drugs can come as pills, liquids, or injections. You usually get a drug from a doctor or a pharmacy.

dry.definition: The time when something loses its water and becomes dry.
dry.exampleSentence: The dry of the clothes took a long time in the cold weather.
dry.contextPassage: We often talk about the dry of wet things like clothes or hair. A warm room helps the dry happen faster.

earn.definition: To get money or a reward because you worked hard for it.
earn.exampleSentence: She will earn more money at her new job.
earn.contextPassage: You earn money by doing work or helping others. You can also earn respect or praise by doing something well.

earth.definition: The planet where all people, animals, and plants live.
earth.exampleSentence: The Earth travels around the sun every year.
earth.contextPassage: Earth is the third planet from the sun in our solar system. It is the only planet we know that has life on it.

easily.definition: In a way that feels comfortable and without any worry or trouble.
easily.exampleSentence: She fell asleep easily after her long day at work.
easily.contextPassage: We use easily to say something did not take much effort. For example, he easily found the answer to the question.

education.definition: The way people learn new things, skills, and knowledge at school or in life.
education.exampleSentence: A good education will help you find a better job.
education.contextPassage: Education usually happens in schools and universities. Parents and teachers are both important parts of a child's education.

either.definition: Used after a negative sentence to mean also or as well.
either.exampleSentence: He doesn't like coffee, and I don't either.
either.contextPassage: We use either at the end of a negative sentence to agree. For example, she can't swim, and her brother can't either.

electric.definition: The supply of electricity that comes into your home or building.
electric.exampleSentence: We didn't have the electric for two hours after the storm.
electric.contextPassage: People sometimes say the electric when they mean the electricity in their home. Paying for the electric is a regular monthly cost.

electrical.definition: A person whose job is to plan and work with electrical systems and equipment.
electrical.exampleSentence: They called an electrical to check the wiring in the building.
electrical.contextPassage: An electrical works with complex power systems in buildings or factories. This job needs special training and knowledge.

electricity.definition: The power that moves through wires and makes lights and machines work.
electricity.exampleSentence: The electricity went off during the big storm last night.
electricity.contextPassage: We use electricity every day to power our lights and phones. Without electricity, the TV and fridge will not work.

electronic.definition: Using or controlled by very small electrical parts inside a device.
electronic.exampleSentence: She got a new electronic watch for her birthday.
electronic.contextPassage: Electronic devices like phones and laptops need power to work. Stores sell many electronic products, such as tablets and cameras.

employee.definition: A person who works for a company or another person and gets paid.
employee.exampleSentence: The new employee started work at the office on Monday.
employee.contextPassage: Every employee at the shop wears a blue uniform. The manager said all employees will get a day off next week.

employer.definition: A person or company that pays workers to do a job.
employer.exampleSentence: Her employer gave her a bonus at the end of the year.
employer.contextPassage: A good employer treats workers fairly and pays them on time. You can ask your employer for more hours if you want to earn more money.

empty.definition: A bottle or other container that has nothing left inside it.
empty.exampleSentence: Please put the empties in the recycling bin outside.
empty.contextPassage: After the party, there were many empties on the table. We collected all the empties and took them to the shop.

ending.definition: The last part of something, like a story or a film.
ending.exampleSentence: I really liked the happy ending of that movie.
ending.contextPassage: The ending of the book surprised everyone in the class. A good ending makes you feel that the story is finished.

energy.definition: The power or strength you need to do things and stay active.
energy.exampleSentence: I had no energy after walking all day in the city.
energy.contextPassage: Eating a good breakfast will give you energy for the morning. Children usually have a lot of energy and love to run and play.

engine.definition: The part of a car, train, or other machine that makes it move.
engine.exampleSentence: The car would not start because the engine was broken.
engine.contextPassage: A mechanic will check the engine to find the problem. The train engine makes a loud noise when it leaves the station.

engineer.definition: A person whose job is to design, build, or fix machines and structures.
engineer.exampleSentence: The engineer fixed the broken bridge in three days.
engineer.contextPassage: You need to study for many years to become an engineer. Engineers design things like roads, buildings, and machines.

enter.definition: The key on a computer keyboard that you press to confirm or start something.
enter.exampleSentence: Press enter after you type in your password.
enter.contextPassage: You can press the enter key to send your message. If you hit enter by mistake, you can sometimes undo the action.

essay.definition: A piece of writing where you share your ideas about one topic.
essay.exampleSentence: The teacher asked us to write an essay about our hometown.
essay.contextPassage: A good essay has a clear beginning, middle, and end. Students often write essays at school to show what they have learned.

everyday.definition: A normal, regular day with nothing special happening.
everyday.exampleSentence: Monday felt like just another everyday with nothing new.
everyday.contextPassage: On an everyday, she wakes up, goes to work, and cooks dinner. Not every day is exciting — most days are just a normal everyday.

everywhere.definition: In all places, or in every place.
everywhere.exampleSentence: She looked everywhere but could not find her keys.
everywhere.contextPassage: You can use 'everywhere' to talk about all the places in an area. For example, there were flowers everywhere in the park.

exact.definition: To force someone to give or do something they do not want to give or do.
exact.exampleSentence: The boss exacted a promise from every worker on the team.
exact.contextPassage: When someone exacts something, they make another person do it or pay it. It often happens when someone has power over another person.

exactly.definition: In a way that is completely correct, with no mistakes or changes.
exactly.exampleSentence: She cut the paper to exactly the right size for the frame.
exactly.contextPassage: You use 'exactly' to say something is 100% correct or true. For example, that is exactly what I wanted to say.

excellent.definition: Very good, much better than most other things.
excellent.exampleSentence: The students got excellent results on their English test.
excellent.contextPassage: You use 'excellent' to show you are very happy with something. A teacher might say 'excellent work' to a student who did very well.

except.definition: To leave someone or something out of a group or rule.
except.exampleSentence: The teacher excepted Tom from the homework because he was sick.
except.contextPassage: When you except someone, you say the rule or plan does not include them. This word is often used in formal or official situations.

exist.definition: To be real and present in the world.
exist.exampleSentence: Dinosaurs did not exist when people first lived on Earth.
exist.contextPassage: You use 'exist' to talk about whether something is real or alive. For example, some people think that life exists on other planets.

expect.definition: To think something will happen in the future.
expect.exampleSentence: I expect my friend will arrive at the station around noon.
expect.contextPassage: You use 'expect' when you feel sure or fairly sure about something coming. For example, she expected the bus to be late again today.

experience.definition: Something that happens to you and that you feel or learn from.
experience.exampleSentence: Living in a new country was a big experience for her.
experience.contextPassage: An experience can be good or bad, but it always teaches you something. Many people say travelling to new places is a great experience.

expert.definition: A person who knows a lot about one subject or can do something very well.
expert.exampleSentence: We called an expert to fix the problem with our computer.
expert.contextPassage: An expert usually has many years of study or work in one area. For example, a cooking expert can show you how to make difficult meals.

explanation.definition: Words that help you understand why something happened or how something works.
explanation.exampleSentence: The teacher gave a clear explanation of the new grammar rule.
explanation.contextPassage: You ask for an explanation when you do not understand something. A good explanation uses simple words and examples to make things clear.

extremely.definition: Very, very much, more than usual or more than expected.
extremely.exampleSentence: It was extremely cold outside, so we stayed inside all day.
extremely.contextPassage: You use 'extremely' to make an adjective stronger than 'very'. For example, the food at that restaurant was extremely good.

factory.definition: A large building where people or machines make products in big numbers.
factory.exampleSentence: My uncle works in a factory that makes shoes every day.
factory.contextPassage: Factories often have many workers and big machines inside them. Many things you buy in shops, like clothes and food, come from a factory.

fail.definition: To not do something well enough, or to not pass a test.
fail.exampleSentence: She studied hard because she did not want to fail the exam.
fail.contextPassage: You can fail a test if you do not prepare well. If you fail, you can try again next time.

fair.definition: Something that treats everyone in an equal and right way.
fair.exampleSentence: The teacher made sure the rules were fair for every student.
fair.contextPassage: People feel happy when things are fair. It is not fair if one person gets more than others for no reason.

fall.definition: The movement of something dropping down to a lower place.
fall.exampleSentence: The fall from the ladder hurt his leg badly.
fall.contextPassage: A fall can happen very quickly and without warning. You should be careful on wet floors to avoid a fall.

fan.definition: A flat object you hold and wave by hand to move air and cool yourself.
fan.exampleSentence: She used a fan to cool her face on the hot day.
fan.contextPassage: A fan is useful when the weather is very warm. You open it up and wave it back and forth near your face.

farm.definition: A piece of land where people grow food or keep animals.
farm.exampleSentence: My grandfather lives on a farm with many cows and chickens.
farm.contextPassage: Farms can be very large or quite small. People on a farm work hard every day to grow food.

farming.definition: To grow crops or keep animals on a farm as work.
farming.exampleSentence: His family has been farming this land for many years.
farming.contextPassage: Farming is hard physical work done outside. People who farm need to know about soil, plants, and animals.

fashion.definition: A popular style of clothes or other things that many people like right now.
fashion.exampleSentence: She always wears the latest fashion from the shops.
fashion.contextPassage: Fashion changes very often, sometimes every season. Something that is popular today may not be in fashion next year.

fat.definition: A soft substance stored in the body of animals and people that holds energy.
fat.exampleSentence: The doctor said he should eat less fat in his meals.
fat.contextPassage: Your body stores fat to use later as energy. Foods like butter and oil contain a lot of fat.

fear.definition: A very strong and unpleasant feeling you get when you think something bad will happen.
fear.exampleSentence: She felt great fear when she heard the loud noise outside.
fear.contextPassage: Fear can make your heart beat very fast. Many people have a fear of things like spiders or the dark.

feed.definition: Food that is given to animals to eat.
feed.exampleSentence: The farmer put feed in the bucket for the horses.
feed.contextPassage: Animals on a farm need feed every day. Chickens, cows, and pigs all eat different kinds of feed.

female.definition: A person or animal that belongs to the sex that can have babies.
female.exampleSentence: The vet said the cat is female and will have kittens soon.
female.contextPassage: Female animals often take care of their young ones. In many forms, you need to say if you are male or female.

fiction.definition: Books and stories about people and events that are not real.
fiction.exampleSentence: She loves reading fiction because the stories are very exciting.
fiction.contextPassage: Fiction comes from a writer's imagination, not from real life. Novels and short stories are common types of fiction.

field.definition: A large open area of land with grass or crops and no buildings or trees.
field.exampleSentence: The children played football in the big green field.
field.contextPassage: A field is usually outside in the countryside. Farmers grow food in fields.

fight.definition: To use your body or weapons against another person to hurt them.
fight.exampleSentence: Two boys started to fight in the school yard.
fight.contextPassage: People fight when they are very angry. In a war, soldiers fight against each other.

film.definition: A very thin layer of something that covers a surface.
film.exampleSentence: There was a thin film of oil on top of the water.
film.contextPassage: A film can cover food or a liquid. You can sometimes see a film of dust on old furniture.

final.definition: The last and most important test at the end of a school class or course.
final.exampleSentence: She studied very hard for her English final.
final.contextPassage: Students take a final at the end of the school year. A good score on the final is very important.

finally.definition: After a long time or after many other things, at the end.
finally.exampleSentence: We waited for one hour and the bus finally came.
finally.contextPassage: You use finally when something takes a long time to happen. It often shows you are happy or relieved that something is done.

finger.definition: One of the four long thin parts of your hand, not including your thumb.
finger.exampleSentence: She cut her finger while she was cooking dinner.
finger.contextPassage: People use their fingers to hold and touch things. You have four fingers and one thumb on each hand.

finish.definition: The end of something, when it stops or is complete.
finish.exampleSentence: Everyone cheered at the finish of the race.
finish.contextPassage: A finish comes at the very end of something. The finish of a race is the line runners cross last.

firstly.definition: Used to introduce the first thing in a list of points or reasons.
firstly.exampleSentence: Firstly, you need to write your name on the paper.
firstly.contextPassage: People use firstly when they have two or more points to say. You can say firstly, then secondly, then thirdly.

fishing.definition: The activity of trying to catch fish from a river, lake, or the sea.
fishing.exampleSentence: My dad goes fishing at the lake every Saturday morning.
fishing.contextPassage: Fishing is a popular hobby for many people. You need a fishing rod and some water to go fishing.

fit.definition: How well something matches or works with something else in size or shape.
fit.exampleSentence: I tried on the jacket but the fit was too small.
fit.contextPassage: You talk about the fit of clothes when you try them on. A good fit means the item is the right size for you.

fix.definition: Something you do to repair a broken or wrong thing and make it work again.
fix.exampleSentence: The plumber did a quick fix on the broken pipe.
fix.contextPassage: A fix solves a problem with something that is broken. Sometimes a fix is fast, but some fixes take a long time.

flat.definition: A large area of land that is very smooth and has no hills or high parts.
flat.exampleSentence: We drove across a wide flat of dry land all day.
flat.contextPassage: A flat is very easy to walk or drive across. Some deserts and farmland areas are large flats.

flu.definition: An illness that makes you feel very tired, hot, and sick, like a bad cold.
flu.exampleSentence: She stayed home because she had the flu.
flu.contextPassage: The flu is common in winter. You should rest and drink water when you have it.

fly.definition: A very small insect with two wings that flies around food and rubbish.
fly.exampleSentence: A fly landed on my sandwich at the park.
fly.contextPassage: Flies are often found near food and rubbish bins. They are very small and move very fast.

flying.definition: Moving through the air, like a bird or a plane.
flying.exampleSentence: The bird was flying over the tall trees.
flying.contextPassage: You can use flying to describe something moving through the air. Birds and planes are both flying when they are in the air.

following.definition: Going behind someone or something in the same direction.
following.exampleSentence: The dog was following its owner down the street.
following.contextPassage: You can say one person is following another when they walk behind them. A shadow also follows you everywhere you go.

foreign.definition: A person who comes from a different country.
foreign.exampleSentence: A foreign student joined our class last week.
foreign.contextPassage: We use foreign to talk about things or people from other countries. For example, a foreign language is one from another country.

forest.definition: A very large area of land with many trees growing close together.
forest.exampleSentence: We went for a walk in the dark green forest.
forest.contextPassage: A forest is bigger than a small group of trees. Many wild animals live in forests.

fork.definition: A tool with a long handle and sharp points, used to pick up food or dig in the ground.
fork.exampleSentence: She used a fork to eat her pasta.
fork.contextPassage: A fork is part of a set of tools used for eating. You usually see a fork, knife, and spoon on a dinner table.

forward.definition: A player in some team sports who plays at the front and tries to score.
forward.exampleSentence: He played as a forward in the football match.
forward.contextPassage: A forward is close to the other team's goal. Their main job is to attack and score points.

free.definition: Something you can have or do without paying any money.
free.exampleSentence: The coffee at the hotel was free every morning.
free.contextPassage: When something is free, you do not need to pay for it. Free things are often given to customers as a gift.

fresh.definition: Food or water that is new, clean, and not old or bad.
fresh.exampleSentence: I bought fresh bread from the bakery this morning.
fresh.contextPassage: Fresh food has not been kept for a long time. Fresh water is clean water that is good to drink.

fridge.definition: A machine in the kitchen that keeps food cold and fresh.
fridge.exampleSentence: He put the milk back in the fridge after breakfast.
fridge.contextPassage: Most people keep food like milk, fruit, and vegetables in the fridge. It keeps food cold so it stays good for longer.

frog.definition: A small animal with soft wet skin and long back legs that it uses to jump.
frog.exampleSentence: We saw a small green frog near the pond.
frog.contextPassage: Frogs live near water and can jump very far. They make a loud sound, especially at night.

furniture.definition: The big objects in a room that you use or sit on, like tables, chairs, and beds.
furniture.exampleSentence: We bought new furniture for the living room.
furniture.contextPassage: Furniture makes a room comfortable and useful. A sofa and a desk are common pieces of furniture.

future.definition: The time that has not happened yet; what will come later.
future.exampleSentence: She wants to be a doctor in the future.
future.contextPassage: We talk about the future when we make plans or dreams. You can use words like 'will' or 'going to' when you talk about the future.

gallery.definition: A building or room where people go to look at paintings and art.
gallery.exampleSentence: We visited a gallery and saw many beautiful paintings.
gallery.contextPassage: A gallery usually shows art to the public. Some galleries are free, and some you have to pay to enter.

gas.definition: Something that is not a liquid or solid, like air, and fills any space around it.
gas.exampleSentence: The balloon is full of gas and floats in the air.
gas.contextPassage: Gas has no fixed shape and spreads out easily. Some homes use gas to heat water or cook food.

gate.definition: A door in a wall or fence outside that you open to go in or out.
gate.exampleSentence: Please close the gate so the dog cannot run away.
gate.contextPassage: A gate is usually at the front of a garden or yard. You often need to open a gate to enter a building or park.

gift.definition: Something you give to another person for free, to make them happy.
gift.exampleSentence: She gave him a gift for his birthday.
gift.contextPassage: People often give gifts on special days like birthdays or holidays. A gift does not have to be expensive to make someone feel good.

goal.definition: Something important that you want to do or achieve.
goal.exampleSentence: His goal is to learn English this year.
goal.contextPassage: A goal gives you something to work towards. Many people set goals at the start of a new year.

god.definition: A very powerful being that people believe created the world and controls everything.
god.exampleSentence: Many people pray to God every day.
god.contextPassage: Different religions have different ideas about God. Some people believe in one God, and some believe in many gods.

gold.definition: A shiny yellow metal that is very valuable and used to make jewellery.
gold.exampleSentence: She wore a beautiful ring made of gold.
gold.contextPassage: Gold is one of the most expensive metals in the world. People also use gold to make medals and coins.

golf.definition: A sport where you hit a small ball into holes in the ground using a stick.
golf.exampleSentence: My dad plays golf every Sunday morning.
golf.contextPassage: A golf course usually has eighteen holes. Players try to hit the ball into each hole using as few hits as possible.

grass.definition: The short green plants that cover the ground in gardens and parks.
grass.exampleSentence: The children sat on the grass in the park.
grass.contextPassage: Grass is the green ground cover you see in gardens and fields. People cut grass to keep it short and tidy.

greet.definition: To say hello to someone in a friendly way when you meet them.
greet.exampleSentence: She went to the door to greet her guests.
greet.contextPassage: You can greet someone with a smile, a wave, or a handshake. It is polite to greet people when they arrive at your home.

ground.definition: The flat surface under your feet outside, made of soil or rock.
ground.exampleSentence: The children sat on the ground and ate their lunch.
ground.contextPassage: We use 'ground' to talk about the surface outside, not inside a building. The dog dug a hole in the ground near the tree.

guest.definition: A person who stays at someone's home or goes to a party because they got an invitation.
guest.exampleSentence: We have a guest staying at our house this weekend.
guest.contextPassage: A guest is someone you invite, not someone who lives with you. My mum cooked a big dinner for our guests.

guide.definition: A person whose job is to show visitors around a place and tell them about it.
guide.exampleSentence: The guide showed us around the old castle.
guide.contextPassage: You often find a guide at museums, cities, or nature parks. Our guide told us many interesting things about the city.

gun.definition: A weapon that shoots small metal objects very fast and can hurt people.
gun.exampleSentence: The police officer carried a gun on his belt.
gun.contextPassage: Guns are used by soldiers and police officers. In some countries, people are not allowed to have a gun.

guy.definition: An informal word for a man or a boy.
guy.exampleSentence: That guy over there is my neighbour.
guy.contextPassage: We often use 'guy' in friendly, everyday conversation. You can also say 'guys' to talk to a group of people.

habit.definition: Something you do often and almost every day without thinking about it.
habit.exampleSentence: She has a habit of drinking coffee every morning.
habit.contextPassage: A habit can be good or bad, like exercising or eating too much sugar. It is hard to stop a habit after a long time.

half.definition: One of two equal parts of something.
half.exampleSentence: I ate half of the pizza and saved the rest.
half.contextPassage: We use 'half' to talk about dividing things into two equal pieces. Half an hour means thirty minutes.

hall.definition: A room or space just inside the front door of a building.
hall.exampleSentence: Please leave your shoes in the hall.
hall.contextPassage: The hall is the first room you walk into when you enter a house. We also use 'hall' for a large room where people meet or have events.

happily.definition: Used to say that something is lucky or good news.
happily.exampleSentence: Happily, the weather was nice on the day of our trip.
happily.contextPassage: We use 'happily' to show that something good happened by luck. Happily, nobody was hurt in the accident.

have auxiliary.definition: A word used with another verb to show that something happened in the past or is finished.
have auxiliary.exampleSentence: I have already eaten my dinner tonight.
have auxiliary.contextPassage: We use 'have' before a past participle to make the present perfect. For example, you can say she has finished her homework.

headache.definition: A pain that you feel inside your head.
headache.exampleSentence: I have a headache so I will rest at home today.
headache.contextPassage: A headache can happen when you are tired or stressed. Many people take medicine to help a headache go away.

heart.definition: The part inside your chest that pumps blood around your body.
heart.exampleSentence: The doctor listened to his heart during the check-up.
heart.contextPassage: Your heart beats faster when you run or feel scared. We also use 'heart' to talk about feelings, like love.

heat.definition: The feeling of something being very warm or hot.
heat.exampleSentence: The heat from the sun made everyone feel very tired.
heat.contextPassage: Heat can come from the sun, a fire, or an oven. On hot days, people drink water to stay cool in the heat.

heavy.definition: A bad person in a story who does wrong or harmful things.
heavy.exampleSentence: The heavy in the movie stole money from the town.
heavy.contextPassage: In stories and films, the heavy is the main bad character. The heavy often tries to stop the hero from winning.

height.definition: How tall something is from the bottom to the top.
height.exampleSentence: The teacher measured the height of each student in class.
height.contextPassage: You can measure the height of a person, a building, or a tree. A very tall building has a great height.

helpful.definition: Ready to give help or support to other people.
helpful.exampleSentence: My neighbor was helpful when I moved into my new house.
helpful.contextPassage: A helpful person makes things easier for others. It is always good to be helpful to your friends and family.

hero.definition: A person who does something very brave to help others.
hero.exampleSentence: The firefighter was a hero after he saved the child.
hero.contextPassage: A hero is often someone who acts without thinking about their own safety. Many stories and films have a hero who fights against bad people.

hers.definition: Something that belongs to a girl or woman.
hers.exampleSentence: That red bag on the chair is hers.
hers.contextPassage: We use hers to show that something belongs to a female. For example, if a girl owns a book, you can say the book is hers.

herself.definition: Used to talk about a girl or woman doing something alone or as the subject of the sentence.
herself.exampleSentence: She painted the whole room herself without any help.
herself.contextPassage: We use herself when the same woman is both doing and receiving the action. For example, she hurt herself means she caused the pain.

hide.definition: To put something in a place where other people cannot find or see it.
hide.exampleSentence: She decided to hide the birthday gift under the bed.
hide.contextPassage: People often hide presents before a birthday or holiday. You can also hide yourself by going behind a wall or a tree.

high.definition: A place or point that is far above the ground or other things.
high.exampleSentence: The plane flew to a new high above the clouds.
high.contextPassage: A high can be a physical place, like the top of a mountain. People feel happy when they reach a high point in their work or sport.

hill.definition: A raised area of land that is smaller than a mountain.
hill.exampleSentence: The children ran up the hill to fly their kite.
hill.contextPassage: A hill is higher than the flat land around it but not as big as a mountain. People often walk or ride bikes up and down hills.

himself.definition: Used to talk about a man or boy doing something alone or as the subject of the sentence.
himself.exampleSentence: He made a big sandwich for himself after school.
himself.contextPassage: We use himself when the same man is both doing and receiving the action. For example, he hurt himself means he caused the pain.

hit.definition: A strong knock when one thing strikes another thing.
hit.exampleSentence: The bat gave the ball a hard hit across the field.
hit.contextPassage: A hit happens when something moves fast and touches another object. In sports like baseball, a good hit can send the ball very far.

hockey.definition: A sport played on ice where two teams try to put a small round object into the other team's net.
hockey.exampleSentence: My brother plays hockey every Saturday at the ice rink.
hockey.contextPassage: Hockey players use long sticks to hit the puck. It is a very fast and popular sport in cold countries.

hold.definition: When you hold something, you keep it firmly in your hand.
hold.exampleSentence: She had a tight hold on her bag on the busy bus.
hold.contextPassage: You can keep a hold on something so it does not fall. A strong hold means you are gripping something very firmly.

hole.definition: An empty space or opening in something solid.
hole.exampleSentence: There is a big hole in my sock near the toe.
hole.contextPassage: A hole can go all the way through something, like in paper. You can also dig a hole in the ground outside.

hope.definition: To want something good to happen and believe it is possible.
hope.exampleSentence: I hope the weather will be sunny for our picnic tomorrow.
hope.contextPassage: People often hope for good news or a good result. You can say you hope something will happen in the future.

huge.definition: Something that is very, very big in size.
huge.exampleSentence: They live in a huge house with ten bedrooms.
huge.contextPassage: You can use huge to talk about big buildings, animals, or crowds. It means something is much bigger than normal.

human.definition: A person, a man, a woman, or a child.
human.exampleSentence: Every human needs water and food to stay alive.
human.contextPassage: We use the word human to talk about people in general. Humans are different from animals because they can speak and think in complex ways.

hurt.definition: A bad feeling inside you after someone does or says something unkind.
hurt.exampleSentence: She felt a deep hurt after her friend forgot her birthday.
hurt.contextPassage: Hurt is the sad or painful feeling you get from unkind words or actions. This kind of hurt is not a physical pain but an emotional one.

ill.definition: Problems or bad things that cause trouble or sadness for people.
ill.exampleSentence: The town faced many ills after the old factory closed down.
ill.contextPassage: Ills are difficult problems that affect a group of people. You often see this word used in the plural form, like social ills.

illness.definition: A sickness or health problem that makes your body or mind feel bad.
illness.exampleSentence: He missed a week of school because of a serious illness.
illness.contextPassage: An illness can be short, like a cold, or long, like a heart problem. When you have an illness, you often need to see a doctor.

image.definition: A picture or photo of something or someone.
image.exampleSentence: She saved a beautiful image of the sunset on her phone.
image.contextPassage: An image can be a photo, a drawing, or a picture on a screen. You can share images with friends online very easily today.

lock.definition: A small metal object on a door or box that you need a key to open.
lock.exampleSentence: He put a new lock on the front door of his house.
lock.contextPassage: You use a lock to keep a door or bag safe and closed. You need the right key to open a lock.

lorry.definition: A large road vehicle used to carry and move heavy goods from place to place.
lorry.exampleSentence: A big lorry stopped outside the shop to deliver new boxes.
lorry.contextPassage: Lorry is the word used mainly in British English. In American English, people call the same vehicle a truck.

lost.definition: You lost something when you had it before but you do not have it now.
lost.exampleSentence: She lost her keys on the way to school.
lost.contextPassage: You can lose things like money, a phone, or a bag. People often say they lost something when they cannot find it.

loud.definition: A loud is a strong, high sound that is easy to hear.
loud.exampleSentence: There was a loud from the kitchen that scared everyone.
loud.contextPassage: A loud can come from music, traffic, or a crowd. It is the opposite of a quiet sound.

loudly.definition: When you do something loudly, you make a lot of noise and people can easily hear you.
loudly.exampleSentence: The children laughed loudly during the funny movie.
loudly.contextPassage: People often speak loudly in noisy places so others can hear them. You can also sing, cry, or clap loudly.

lovely.definition: A lovely is a person who looks very attractive and beautiful.
lovely.exampleSentence: The photographer took pictures of the lovelies at the fashion show.
lovely.contextPassage: The word lovely is often used to talk about models or beautiful people. It can also describe someone who looks very nice.

low.definition: A low is a point or time when something is at its smallest or worst level.
low.exampleSentence: The temperature reached a new low last night.
low.contextPassage: You can talk about a low in weather, prices, or feelings. A low is the opposite of a high.

luck.definition: Luck is when something good happens to you by chance and not because you planned it.
luck.exampleSentence: He found twenty dollars on the street — what luck!
luck.contextPassage: People often wish each other good luck before a test or a game. Luck is something you cannot control.

lucky.definition: A lucky person has good things happen to them by chance.
lucky.exampleSentence: She was lucky to find a seat on the busy train.
lucky.contextPassage: People say you are lucky when something good happens without a reason. You can feel lucky when you win something or avoid a problem.

mail.definition: A mail is a bag or wallet used to carry letters and packages.
mail.exampleSentence: The worker picked up the heavy mail full of letters.
mail.contextPassage: A mail bag is used to move post from one place to another. Postal workers often carry a mail bag on their route.

male.definition: A male is a person or animal that is a boy or a man.
male.exampleSentence: The doctor said the baby was a male.
male.contextPassage: The word male can describe a person, an animal, or a bird. It is the opposite of female.

manage.definition: A manage is the act of looking after or controlling something or someone.
manage.exampleSentence: Good manage of the shop helped it grow quickly.
manage.contextPassage: A manage means keeping things organised and running well. It is the work of making sure everything goes the right way.

manager.definition: A manager is a person whose job is to be in charge of a business, a team, or a place.
manager.exampleSentence: The manager opened the store every morning at nine.
manager.contextPassage: A manager makes decisions and helps workers do their jobs well. You can find a manager in a shop, a restaurant, or a sports team.

mark.definition: A mark is a line or area that shows where something begins or ends.
mark.exampleSentence: The referee checked that the ball crossed the mark.
