mark.contextPassage: A mark can show a border or a dividing line on the ground. People use marks to know where one area stops and another one starts.

marry.definition: To become the husband or wife of someone you love.
marry.exampleSentence: She will marry her boyfriend next summer.
marry.contextPassage: People often marry in a church or a garden. You can also get married at a local office.

mathematics.definition: The school subject where you study numbers, shapes, and how they work together.
mathematics.exampleSentence: Mathematics is her favourite subject at school.
mathematics.contextPassage: You use mathematics to add, subtract, and solve problems. It is an important subject in every school.

maths.definition: The short word for mathematics, the study of numbers and shapes.
maths.exampleSentence: He is really good at maths this year.
maths.contextPassage: Many students find maths difficult at first. Teachers use games to make maths more fun.

matter.definition: The physical material that everything around us is made of.
matter.exampleSentence: Water is a type of matter that we drink every day.
matter.contextPassage: All objects are made of matter. Solids, liquids, and gases are all types of matter.

may.definition: Used to say that something is possible or allowed.
may.exampleSentence: You may leave the classroom after the test.
may.contextPassage: We use may to ask for permission politely. You can also use may to say something is possible.

medical.definition: A check-up where a doctor looks at your body to see if you are healthy.
medical.exampleSentence: He had a medical before starting his new job.
medical.contextPassage: A medical can include simple tests like checking your heart. Doctors do a medical to find any health problems early.

medicine.definition: A pill, liquid, or tablet that helps your body get better when you are sick.
medicine.exampleSentence: The doctor gave her medicine for her bad cold.
medicine.contextPassage: You should take medicine at the right time each day. Always follow the doctor's instructions when you take medicine.

memory.definition: The ability to remember things that happened in the past.
memory.exampleSentence: My grandfather has a very good memory for names.
memory.contextPassage: A good memory helps you learn new words faster. Some games are good for training your memory.

metal.definition: A hard, strong material like iron, gold, or steel.
metal.exampleSentence: The chair legs are made of metal.
metal.contextPassage: Metal is used to build cars, bridges, and tools. It is usually shiny and can feel cold to touch.

middle.definition: The part that is at the centre, not at the start or the end.
middle.exampleSentence: She sat in the middle of the back row.
middle.contextPassage: The middle of a room is the area farthest from the walls. You can cut something in the middle to make two equal parts.

might.definition: Great power or strength that a person or group has.
might.exampleSentence: The soldiers fought with all their might.
might.contextPassage: People with great might can do very hard physical things. In stories, kings and heroes often show their might in battle.

mind.definition: The part of you that thinks, remembers, and makes decisions.
mind.exampleSentence: She has a very quick mind and learns fast.
mind.contextPassage: Your mind helps you solve problems and make choices. Reading books is a great way to keep your mind active.

mine.definition: The thing or things that belong to me, not to anyone else.
mine.exampleSentence: That blue bag is mine, not yours.
mine.contextPassage: You use 'mine' so you do not have to repeat a noun. For example, you can say 'your car and mine'.

mirror.definition: A flat glass object that shows you your own face and body when you look at it.
mirror.exampleSentence: She looked in the mirror before leaving the house.
mirror.contextPassage: People use a mirror to check how they look. You can find a mirror in a bathroom or bedroom.

missing.definition: To fail to hit or reach something you are aiming at.
missing.exampleSentence: He kicked the ball but kept missing the goal.
missing.contextPassage: A player can be missing the target again and again. This word is often used in sports.

mobile.definition: A decoration made of small objects hanging on strings that move in the air.
mobile.exampleSentence: There was a colorful mobile hanging above the baby's bed.
mobile.contextPassage: Mobiles are popular in children's rooms. The small objects move when there is wind or air.

monkey.definition: A small wild animal with a long tail that climbs trees and lives in hot places.
monkey.exampleSentence: We saw a monkey jumping between the trees at the zoo.
monkey.contextPassage: Monkeys are very active and playful animals. They eat fruit and live in groups.

moon.definition: The large round object in the sky that goes around the Earth and shines at night.
moon.exampleSentence: The moon was very bright and round last night.
moon.contextPassage: You can see the moon in the sky after dark. It looks different each week of the month.

mostly.definition: Almost all of the time, or almost all of something.
mostly.exampleSentence: The students in the class were mostly from the same city.
mostly.contextPassage: You use 'mostly' to say that something is true in most cases. For example, the weather is mostly warm in summer.

motorcycle.definition: A fast two-wheeled vehicle with an engine that one or two people can ride.
motorcycle.exampleSentence: My brother rides his motorcycle to work every day.
motorcycle.contextPassage: A motorcycle goes faster than a bicycle. You need a special driving licence to ride one.

musical.definition: A show or film where people sing and dance to tell a story.
musical.exampleSentence: We went to see a musical at the theatre last Saturday.
musical.contextPassage: Musicals are popular in big cities. The actors in a musical sing many songs during the show.

musician.definition: A person who plays a musical instrument or sings as a job or hobby.
musician.exampleSentence: The musician played the guitar on the small outdoor stage.
musician.contextPassage: A musician can perform alone or with a group. Some musicians play at weddings or concerts.

myself.definition: Used when you are the person doing something and also the person it happens to.
myself.exampleSentence: I made myself a cup of tea this morning.
myself.contextPassage: You use 'myself' to show that you did something without help. For example, 'I fixed the chair myself'.

narrow.definition: A thin passage of water that connects two larger areas of water.
narrow.exampleSentence: The boat moved slowly through the narrow between the two islands.
narrow.contextPassage: A narrow can be difficult to sail through. Sailors have to be careful in narrow passages.

national.definition: A person who belongs to a particular country.
national.exampleSentence: She is a French national living in London.
national.contextPassage: A national has the rights of their home country. You need a passport to show you are a national of your country.

nature.definition: All the plants, animals, and land that exist outside of cities and towns.
nature.exampleSentence: We walked in the forest and enjoyed the nature around us.
nature.contextPassage: Nature includes trees, rivers, mountains, and wild animals. Many people go outside to relax and spend time in nature.

nearly.definition: Almost, but not completely or not quite.
nearly.exampleSentence: I nearly missed the bus this morning.
nearly.contextPassage: You use nearly when something is very close to being true. For example, it is nearly eight o'clock means a few minutes before eight.

neck.definition: The part of your body between your head and your shoulders.
neck.exampleSentence: She wore a beautiful necklace around her neck.
neck.contextPassage: Your neck lets you turn your head left and right. If you sit at a computer too long, your neck can hurt.

neither.definition: Also not; used to say the same negative thing about a second person or thing.
neither.exampleSentence: I don't like coffee, and neither does my sister.
neither.contextPassage: You use neither after someone says something negative. It shows that the same is true for you or someone else.

nervous.definition: Feeling worried or a little scared about something.
nervous.exampleSentence: She felt nervous before her job interview.
nervous.contextPassage: People often feel nervous before a big test or a new experience. When you are nervous, your heart can beat faster.

noise.definition: A sound that is loud or not pleasant to hear.
noise.exampleSentence: The traffic outside made a lot of noise all night.
noise.contextPassage: Noise can make it hard to sleep or think clearly. People sometimes use earplugs to block out noise.

noisy.definition: Making a lot of loud or unpleasant sounds.
noisy.exampleSentence: The classroom was very noisy before the teacher arrived.
noisy.contextPassage: A noisy place can be hard to work in. Busy restaurants and markets are often very noisy.

none.definition: Not one; not any amount of something.
none.exampleSentence: I wanted some cake, but there was none left.
none.contextPassage: You use none to say that there is zero of something. For example, none of my friends came to the party.

normal.definition: Something that is usual or expected, not special or different.
normal.exampleSentence: It is normal to feel tired after a long day.
normal.contextPassage: When something is normal, it happens in a regular or common way. For example, cold weather in winter is normal in many places.

normally.definition: In the usual way; most of the time.
normally.exampleSentence: I normally wake up at seven o'clock every morning.
normally.contextPassage: You use normally to talk about what you usually do. For example, she normally takes the bus to work.

notice.definition: When you see or become aware of something around you.
notice.exampleSentence: Did you notice the new paint on the walls?
notice.contextPassage: You notice something when you pay attention to it. Sometimes you notice small changes that other people miss.

nowhere.definition: Not in any place; not going to any place.
nowhere.exampleSentence: I had no map, so I went nowhere new.
nowhere.contextPassage: You can use 'nowhere' when you did not go to any place. For example, you stayed home all day and went nowhere.

nut.definition: A small, hard seed with a hard shell that you can often eat.
nut.exampleSentence: She put a nut in her mouth and started to chew.
nut.contextPassage: Nuts grow on trees and plants. Almonds, walnuts, and peanuts are common types of nuts.

ocean.definition: A very large area of salt water between continents.
ocean.exampleSentence: The ship sailed across the ocean for many days.
ocean.contextPassage: The Pacific and Atlantic are large oceans. Oceans cover most of the Earth's surface.

offer.definition: Something you say to give or do something for someone.
offer.exampleSentence: He made an offer to help me move the boxes.
offer.contextPassage: You can accept or reject an offer. A shop can make a special offer with a lower price.

officer.definition: A person with an important and official job, like in the police or army.
officer.exampleSentence: The police officer stopped the car on the road.
officer.contextPassage: An officer has the power to give orders to others. You can find officers in the police, the army, or the government.

oil.definition: A thick liquid fat used for cooking or for machines.
oil.exampleSentence: She put some oil in the pan before cooking the fish.
oil.contextPassage: Olive oil and sunflower oil are common in cooking. Machines also need oil to work well and smoothly.

onto.definition: Moving to a position on top of or on something.
onto.exampleSentence: The cat jumped onto the table and sat down.
onto.contextPassage: You use 'onto' to show movement toward a surface. For example, you can step onto a bus or climb onto a chair.

ordinary.definition: Normal and not special or different from others.
ordinary.exampleSentence: It was just an ordinary day at school for me.
ordinary.contextPassage: Ordinary things are common and happen every day. The opposite of ordinary is special or unusual.

organize.definition: To plan and arrange things so they work well together.
organize.exampleSentence: She will organize all the books on the shelf today.
organize.contextPassage: You can organize a meeting, a party, or your room. When you organize things, you put them in a clear and useful order.

ourselves.definition: Used when we do something to ourselves or by ourselves.
ourselves.exampleSentence: We cooked the dinner ourselves without any help.
ourselves.contextPassage: You use 'ourselves' when the subject and the object are both 'we'. For example, we enjoyed ourselves at the party.

oven.definition: A box inside a cooker that gets hot and is used for baking or heating food.
oven.exampleSentence: She put the cake in the oven for thirty minutes.
oven.contextPassage: You bake bread and cook pizza in an oven. Always be careful because the oven gets very hot inside.

own.definition: Belonging to you and not to anyone else.
own.exampleSentence: He has his own room in the new apartment.
own.contextPassage: You use 'own' to show something belongs to that person. For example, she has her own car and drives to work.

owner.definition: A person who has something that belongs to them.
owner.exampleSentence: The owner of the dog came to the park.
owner.contextPassage: You can ask the owner if you want to buy the shop. The owner is responsible for the building.

pack.definition: A bag or bundle of things you carry on your back.
pack.exampleSentence: She put her clothes in a pack for the trip.
pack.contextPassage: Hikers often use a pack to carry food and water. A pack can be heavy if it has too many things inside.

pain.definition: A bad feeling in your body when you are hurt or sick.
pain.exampleSentence: He felt a strong pain in his leg after the fall.
pain.contextPassage: Pain can come from an injury or illness. You can take medicine to help with pain.

painter.definition: A person whose job is to make pictures using paint.
painter.exampleSentence: The painter made a beautiful picture of the sea.
painter.contextPassage: A painter uses brushes and colors to create art. Some painters sell their pictures in galleries.

palace.definition: A very large and grand building where a king or queen lives.
palace.exampleSentence: The tourists took photos of the old palace.
palace.contextPassage: A palace is usually very big and beautiful. Many palaces are open for visitors to see inside.

pants.definition: A piece of clothing that covers your legs and goes up to your waist.
pants.exampleSentence: He wore blue pants and a white shirt to work.
pants.contextPassage: Pants come in many styles and colors. You can wear pants in cold weather to keep your legs warm.

parking.definition: The act of stopping and leaving a car in a place.
parking.exampleSentence: She found a parking space near the shop.
parking.contextPassage: Parking in the city can be very difficult. Some places have a parking fee that you need to pay.

pass.definition: To move past something or someone, or to give something to a person.
pass.exampleSentence: Can you pass me the salt, please?
pass.contextPassage: You can pass another car on the road carefully. Students need to pass an exam to get a grade.

passenger.definition: A person who travels in a car, bus, train, or plane but does not drive it.
passenger.exampleSentence: The passenger sat by the window on the train.
passenger.contextPassage: A passenger does not control the vehicle. There were many passengers on the bus this morning.

past.definition: The time that already happened before now.
past.exampleSentence: In the past, people did not have mobile phones.
past.contextPassage: We use 'past' to talk about things that are finished. You can learn a lot from events in the past.

patient.definition: A person who gets medical care from a doctor or nurse.
patient.exampleSentence: The patient waited in the doctor's office.
patient.contextPassage: A patient can stay in the hospital when they are very sick. Doctors and nurses help every patient feel better.

pattern.definition: A design with repeated shapes or colors on something.
pattern.exampleSentence: Her dress had a pretty flower pattern on it.
pattern.contextPassage: You can find patterns on clothes, wallpaper, and rugs. A pattern usually repeats the same shape many times.

pay.definition: The money you get from your job for the work you do.
pay.exampleSentence: She gets her pay at the end of every month.
pay.contextPassage: Pay is the money your boss gives you for working. People often check their pay to make sure the amount is correct.

peace.definition: A quiet and calm time when there is no fighting or trouble.
peace.exampleSentence: The two countries signed an agreement to keep the peace.
peace.contextPassage: Peace means no war and no violence between people or countries. People feel safe and happy when there is peace around them.

penny.definition: A small coin with a low value, used in the UK and some other countries.
penny.exampleSentence: She found an old penny on the floor near the door.
penny.contextPassage: A penny is worth very little money on its own. In the UK, people use pennies to pay for small things or make exact change.

per.definition: For each one, used to show a rate or amount.
per.exampleSentence: The bus ticket costs two dollars per person.
per.contextPassage: You use per when you talk about a price or amount for one thing. For example, you can say how much something costs per day or per hour.

per cent.definition: A number that shows how many parts out of one hundred something is.
per cent.exampleSentence: Fifty per cent of the students passed the test.
per cent.contextPassage: Per cent means out of one hundred. You often see per cent used with numbers to talk about discounts, results, or statistics.

perhaps.definition: A word that means something might happen but you are not sure.
perhaps.exampleSentence: Perhaps we will go to the beach tomorrow if it is sunny.
perhaps.contextPassage: You use perhaps when you are not certain about something. It is a polite way to say that something is possible but not definite.

permission.definition: When someone in charge says it is okay for you to do something.
permission.exampleSentence: She asked her teacher for permission to leave the classroom early.
permission.contextPassage: You need permission from someone who has authority, like a parent or boss. Without permission, you are not allowed to do the thing you want to do.

pet.definition: An animal that you keep at home and take care of as a friend.
pet.exampleSentence: My pet cat sleeps on my bed every night.
pet.contextPassage: Common pets include dogs, cats, rabbits, and fish. People love their pets and give them food, water, and attention every day.

petrol.definition: A liquid fuel that people put in cars and other vehicles to make them go.
petrol.exampleSentence: He stopped at the garage to buy some petrol for his car.
petrol.contextPassage: Cars need petrol to run, so drivers fill up their tank at a petrol station. Petrol prices can go up and down from week to week.

photograph.definition: A picture taken with a camera that shows a real person, place, or thing.
photograph.exampleSentence: She took a photograph of her family at the party.
photograph.contextPassage: You can take a photograph with a camera or a phone. People often save their photographs to remember happy moments in their life.

physics.definition: A school subject where you learn about energy, light, sound, and how things move.
physics.exampleSentence: We learned about electricity in our physics class today.
physics.contextPassage: Physics helps you understand how the world around you works. It is one of the science subjects that students study at school.

pick.definition: A heavy tool with a sharp metal point, used to break hard ground or rock.
pick.exampleSentence: The worker used a pick to break up the old road.
pick.contextPassage: A pick is a tool that workers use outside for hard jobs. It has a long handle and a sharp metal head that hits the ground hard.

pilot.definition: A person who flies an airplane or controls a large boat.
pilot.exampleSentence: The pilot landed the plane safely at the airport.
pilot.contextPassage: A pilot works in the sky or on the water. We use this word for the person in control of the vehicle.

planet.definition: A very large, round object in space that moves around a star.
planet.exampleSentence: Earth is the planet where all people live.
planet.contextPassage: Our sun has eight planets moving around it. Mars and Jupiter are also planets in our solar system.

plant.definition: A living thing that grows in soil and is not an animal, like a flower or grass.
plant.exampleSentence: She put a small plant on her bedroom window.
plant.contextPassage: Plants need water and light to grow. Trees and flowers are both types of plants.

plastic.definition: A light, strong material made by people, used to make many everyday objects.
plastic.exampleSentence: He carried his lunch in a plastic bag.
plastic.contextPassage: Many bottles and toys are made of plastic. Plastic is cheap and easy to make into different shapes.

plate.definition: A flat, round dish that you put food on to eat.
plate.exampleSentence: She put the hot pasta on a clean plate.
plate.contextPassage: We use a plate at every meal. You can wash plates in the sink or in a dishwasher.

platform.definition: A raised flat area where people stand, for example at a train station or on a stage.
platform.exampleSentence: We waited on the platform for the next train.
platform.contextPassage: At a train station, you stand on the platform to get on the train. A singer can also perform on a platform in front of an audience.

pleased.definition: Feeling happy because something good happened or someone did something nice.
pleased.exampleSentence: She was very pleased with her test results.
pleased.contextPassage: You can say you are pleased when you feel satisfied and happy. For example, a teacher is pleased when students do well.

pocket.definition: A small bag sewn into clothing where you can keep small things.
pocket.exampleSentence: He put his keys in his jacket pocket.
pocket.contextPassage: Most trousers and jackets have pockets. You can keep your phone or some money in a pocket.

polite.definition: Behaving in a kind and respectful way to other people.
polite.exampleSentence: She was very polite and said thank you to everyone.
polite.contextPassage: It is polite to say please and thank you. Teachers and parents often tell children to be polite.

pollution.definition: Dirty or harmful things added to air, water, or land that make it bad for living things.
pollution.exampleSentence: The river had a lot of pollution from the old factory.
pollution.contextPassage: Cars and factories can cause air pollution. Pollution is bad for animals, plants, and people.

pop.definition: A short, loud sound like the sound a bottle makes when you open it.
pop.exampleSentence: The balloon made a loud pop when it burst.
pop.contextPassage: You can hear a pop when you open a fizzy drink bottle. It is a quick, sharp sound that happens very fast.

population.definition: The total number of people who live in a place, like a city or country.
population.exampleSentence: The population of that city is about one million people.
population.contextPassage: A big country usually has a large population. You can talk about the population of a town, country, or the whole world.

poster.definition: A large picture or notice that you put on a wall.
poster.exampleSentence: She put a poster of her favourite singer on her bedroom wall.
poster.contextPassage: Posters are often used to tell people about events or concerts. You can also use a poster to decorate your room.

power.definition: The ability or strength to do something.
power.exampleSentence: The strong wind cut the power to our house last night.
power.contextPassage: People often talk about power when electricity stops working. You can also use this word to describe how strong a person or machine is.

present.definition: The time that is happening now, not the past or future.
present.exampleSentence: It is important to think about the present, not just the future.
present.contextPassage: People use the word present to talk about what is happening right now. For example, at the present time, she is studying English.

president.definition: The leader of a country or a large organisation.
president.exampleSentence: The president gave a speech to the whole country on TV.
president.contextPassage: In many countries, people vote to choose their president. The president is usually the most important person in the government.

print.definition: Words or pictures that are made on paper by a machine.
print.exampleSentence: I like to read the news in print, not on my phone.
print.contextPassage: Books, newspapers, and magazines are all types of print. Some people say that print is less popular today because of the internet.

printer.definition: A machine that puts words or pictures onto paper.
printer.exampleSentence: I used the printer to make a copy of my homework.
printer.contextPassage: Many offices and homes have a printer near the computer. You need ink inside the printer to make it work.

prison.definition: A building where people must stay as a punishment for a crime.
prison.exampleSentence: The judge sent the man to prison for three years.
prison.contextPassage: People who break serious laws can go to prison. Guards work at a prison to make sure no one leaves.

prize.definition: Something valuable that you get when you win a competition.
prize.exampleSentence: She won first prize in the school drawing competition.
prize.contextPassage: Prizes can be money, a trophy, or a gift. Many people enter competitions because they want to win a prize.

professor.definition: A senior teacher at a university who knows a lot about one subject.
professor.exampleSentence: The professor taught us about history every Monday morning.
professor.contextPassage: Students at university often call their teacher a professor. A professor usually does a lot of research about their subject.

profile.definition: A short description of a person that gives basic information about them.
profile.exampleSentence: She wrote a short profile about herself on the website.
profile.contextPassage: Many social media websites ask you to create a profile. Your profile usually includes your name, photo, and some facts about you.

program.definition: A plan with a set of activities organised in order.
program.exampleSentence: The school started a new program to help students learn maths.
program.contextPassage: A program usually has a clear goal and a list of steps. Many companies and schools create programs to teach new skills.

promise.definition: When you tell someone that you will definitely do something.
promise.exampleSentence: He made a promise to call his mum every Sunday.
promise.contextPassage: When you make a promise, people expect you to keep it. It is important not to break a promise because it can hurt trust.

pronounce.definition: To say officially that something is true or decided.
pronounce.exampleSentence: The judge will pronounce his decision in the morning.
pronounce.contextPassage: A doctor can pronounce someone healthy after a check. A leader can pronounce a new rule for everyone.

pub.definition: A place where people can buy and drink alcohol and food.
pub.exampleSentence: We met our friends at the pub after work.
pub.contextPassage: A pub is a common place to meet friends in the UK. Many pubs also serve hot food and show sports on TV.

public.definition: All the people in a country or area together.
public.exampleSentence: The park is open to the public every day.
public.contextPassage: The public can use free services like libraries and parks. Politicians often listen to what the public wants.

pull.definition: The act of using force to move something toward you.
pull.exampleSentence: He gave the rope a strong pull to open the door.
pull.contextPassage: A pull is the opposite of a push. You need a good pull to open a heavy drawer.

push.definition: The act of pressing something away from you with force.
push.exampleSentence: She gave the box a push and it moved across the floor.
push.contextPassage: A push helps you move heavy things without lifting them. One strong push was enough to open the gate.

queen.definition: A woman who rules a country, or the wife of a king.
queen.exampleSentence: The queen visited the city and people came to see her.
queen.contextPassage: A queen can rule a country for many years. People often cheer when the queen appears in public.

quietly.definition: In a way that makes little or no noise.
quietly.exampleSentence: She walked quietly into the room so no one woke up.
quietly.contextPassage: You should speak quietly in a library. The children quietly sat down when the teacher came in.

race.definition: A competition to see who can finish first.
race.exampleSentence: Tom won the race and got a gold medal.
race.contextPassage: A race can be between people, cars, or animals. Everyone was excited to watch the final race at the stadium.

railway.definition: A system of tracks and trains that carry people or goods.
railway.exampleSentence: We took the railway to travel to the next city.
railway.contextPassage: A railway station is where trains stop to pick up passengers. Many people use the railway every day to get to work.

raise.definition: An increase in the amount of money you earn at work.
raise.exampleSentence: She was very happy when she got a raise at work.
raise.contextPassage: A raise means your employer pays you more money. Many workers ask for a raise after one year in a job.

reach.definition: The act of stretching your arm to touch or get something.
reach.exampleSentence: She needed a chair to reach the top shelf.
reach.contextPassage: A long reach helps you get things that are high up. The child stood on a step to make her reach longer.

receive.definition: The act of getting data or a signal sent to you.
receive.exampleSentence: The phone showed a message about a receive error.
receive.contextPassage: A receive happens when your device gets information from another. You can check the receive status in your app settings.

recent.definition: Something that happened not long ago, close to now.
recent.exampleSentence: Did you see the recent news about the big storm?
recent.contextPassage: You use 'recent' to talk about things that happened in the last few days or weeks. A common phrase is 'recent news' or 'recent changes'.

recently.definition: Not long ago, a short time before now.
recently.exampleSentence: I recently bought a new phone at the mall.
recently.contextPassage: You use 'recently' with past actions that happened very close to today. For example, 'I recently started a new job.'

reception.definition: The area near the front door of a hotel or office where people check in or ask for help.
reception.exampleSentence: Please leave your key at the reception when you check out.
reception.contextPassage: Hotels and offices usually have a reception desk. The person working there can answer your questions.

recipe.definition: A list of food items and steps that tell you how to make a meal.
recipe.exampleSentence: My mother gave me a recipe for chocolate cake.
recipe.contextPassage: You follow a recipe step by step to cook something. Recipes tell you what ingredients you need and how long to cook them.

recognize.definition: To see someone or something and know who or what they are from before.
recognize.exampleSentence: I did not recognize my old friend at first.
recognize.contextPassage: You recognize someone because you have seen them before. It can be hard to recognize people after a long time.

recommend.definition: To tell someone that a thing or place is good and they should try it.
recommend.exampleSentence: Can you recommend a good restaurant near here?
recommend.contextPassage: Friends often recommend movies, books, or places to each other. When you recommend something, you think other people will like it too.

record.definition: A round, flat disc that plays music on a special machine.
record.exampleSentence: My grandfather has many old records in his living room.
record.contextPassage: People played records before CDs and music apps became popular. You need a record player to listen to a record.

recording.definition: Saving sound or video so you can listen to or watch it again later.
recording.exampleSentence: She is recording the concert on her phone right now.
recording.contextPassage: You can make a recording of a song, a class, or a meeting. A recording lets you go back and listen again.

recycle.definition: The act of putting used things through a process so they can be used again.
recycle.exampleSentence: We did a recycle of all the old paper from the office.
recycle.contextPassage: A recycle of bottles and cans helps the environment. Many cities have a recycle programme for people at home.

reduce.definition: To make something smaller in size or amount than it was before.
reduce.exampleSentence: We need to reduce the amount of sugar in our food.
reduce.contextPassage: You can reduce waste by buying less. Turning off lights helps reduce your electricity bill.

refuse.definition: Old or unwanted things that people throw away; rubbish or trash.
refuse.exampleSentence: The workers collected the refuse from every street in town.
refuse.contextPassage: Refuse is taken away by rubbish trucks. Cities have rules about where and when to leave your refuse outside.

region.definition: A large area of land that has something special about it, like its weather or culture.
region.exampleSentence: This region gets a lot of snow in the winter.
region.contextPassage: A country can have many different regions. People in the same region often share the same food and traditions.

regular.definition: A soldier who works full-time in the main army, not a part-time soldier.
regular.exampleSentence: The regular trained every day at the army base.
regular.contextPassage: A regular is a full-time soldier in the national army. People sometimes use this word to show the difference between full-time and part-time soldiers.

relationship.definition: The way two people or things are connected to each other.
relationship.exampleSentence: She has a good relationship with her teacher.
relationship.contextPassage: You can use relationship to talk about family, friends, or work. People often ask about the relationship between two things or two people.

remove.definition: The action of taking something away from a place.
remove.exampleSentence: The remove of the old chairs took about one hour.
remove.contextPassage: Remove as a noun means the act of taking something away. You can talk about the remove of dirt, furniture, or other things.

repair.definition: The work you do to fix something that is broken or damaged.
repair.exampleSentence: The repair of the old bike cost twenty dollars.
repair.contextPassage: You can use repair to talk about fixing a car, a house, or a machine. People often say the repair was fast or very expensive.

replace.definition: To put something back in the place where it was before.
replace.exampleSentence: She will replace the book on the shelf after reading it.
replace.contextPassage: You replace something when you put it back where it was. You can also replace something old with something new.

reply.definition: Something you say or write back to someone after they speak or write to you.
reply.exampleSentence: I got a reply to my email this morning.
reply.contextPassage: A reply can be a spoken answer or a written message. People often wait for a reply after they send a letter or an email.

report.definition: A piece of writing or spoken information that tells people about something that happened.
report.exampleSentence: The teacher read the report about the school trip.
report.contextPassage: A report can be written by a student, a journalist, or a worker. Reports are often about news, school work, or things at a job.

reporter.definition: A person whose job is to find news and tell people about it.
reporter.exampleSentence: The reporter talked to people after the big storm.
reporter.contextPassage: A reporter works for a newspaper, a TV channel, or a website. Reporters go to places where something happens and write or talk about it.

request.definition: When you ask for something in a polite way.
request.exampleSentence: She made a request for a window seat on the plane.
request.contextPassage: A request is a polite way to ask for something you need or want. People make requests at hotels, restaurants, or at work.

research.definition: The work of studying a topic carefully to find new information.
research.exampleSentence: The doctor did research to find a better medicine.
research.contextPassage: Research means looking carefully at a topic to learn more about it. Students and scientists do research to find answers to their questions.

respond.definition: To say or do something as an answer to what someone said or did.
respond.exampleSentence: She did not respond to my question in class.
respond.contextPassage: You respond when you answer a person or react to something. You can respond by speaking, writing, or doing something.

response.definition: Something you say, write, or do as an answer to someone or something.
response.exampleSentence: His response to the question was very short.
response.contextPassage: A response can be spoken or written. People give a response after a question, a letter, or a problem.

rest.definition: The time when you stop working or doing something and relax.
rest.exampleSentence: She sat down to rest after a long walk.
rest.contextPassage: You need rest when you feel tired. A short rest can help you feel better.

review.definition: A piece of writing that gives your opinion about a book, film, or restaurant.
review.exampleSentence: She wrote a review about the new restaurant in town.
review.contextPassage: People often read reviews before they choose a film to watch. A good review can help others decide what to buy.

ride.definition: A trip on a bicycle, horse, or in a vehicle.
ride.exampleSentence: We went for a ride in the car on Sunday.
ride.contextPassage: A bike ride is a great way to enjoy the park. The ride to the city took about one hour.

ring.definition: A small round object that you wear on your finger.
ring.exampleSentence: She got a gold ring as a birthday gift.
ring.contextPassage: People often wear a ring on their wedding day. A ring can be made of gold or silver.

rise.definition: To move up from a lower place to a higher place.
rise.exampleSentence: The sun will rise at six o'clock tomorrow morning.
rise.contextPassage: Hot air rises to the top of a room. The water level in the river rose after the rain.

rock.definition: A hard, natural material that you find in the ground or on mountains.
rock.exampleSentence: He picked up a small rock from the ground.
rock.contextPassage: Some beaches have rocks instead of sand. Rocks can be many different sizes and colors.

role.definition: The character that an actor plays in a film or a play.
role.exampleSentence: She played the role of the queen in the school play.
role.contextPassage: The main role in a film is called the lead role. He practiced a lot to prepare for his role.

roof.definition: The top part of a building that covers it from outside.
roof.exampleSentence: There is a cat sitting on the roof of our house.
roof.contextPassage: A roof keeps the rain out of a building. The strong wind damaged the roof last night.

round.definition: Moving in a circle or going around something.
round.exampleSentence: The children ran round the tree in the garden.
round.contextPassage: The Earth moves round the sun every year. She looked round the room to find her bag.

route.definition: The path or road you take to get from one place to another.
route.exampleSentence: We took a different route to avoid the traffic.
route.contextPassage: The bus route goes through the city center. You can choose a short or long route to school.

rubbish.definition: Things that you do not need and throw away.
rubbish.exampleSentence: Please put the rubbish in the bin outside.
rubbish.contextPassage: It is important to throw your rubbish in the correct place. The rubbish truck comes every Monday morning.

rude.definition: Not polite and not kind to other people.
rude.exampleSentence: It is rude to talk loudly on the phone in a quiet place.
rude.contextPassage: Some people are rude because they are in a bad mood. It is never nice to be rude to others.

runner.definition: A person who moves fast on their feet, often in a race or for exercise.
runner.exampleSentence: The runner crossed the finish line first.
runner.contextPassage: A runner trains every day to stay fast and healthy. You can see runners in parks early in the morning.

running.definition: Moving fast on your feet, faster than walking.
running.exampleSentence: She is running to catch the bus.
running.contextPassage: Running is good exercise for your body. Many people go running in the morning before work.

sadly.definition: In a way that shows you feel unhappy or upset.
sadly.exampleSentence: He sadly said goodbye to his old friends.
sadly.contextPassage: You say something sadly when you feel bad about it. She sadly told us the dog was gone.

speech.definition: The ability to say words and talk to other people.
speech.exampleSentence: The teacher gave a short speech to the class.
speech.contextPassage: A speech is when one person talks to a group. People often give a speech at a wedding or school event.

speed.definition: How fast something or someone moves.
speed.exampleSentence: The car moved at a very high speed.
speed.contextPassage: Speed tells you how quickly something travels. A fast train has a higher speed than a slow bus.

spider.definition: A small animal with eight legs that often makes a web to catch food.
spider.exampleSentence: There is a big spider on the wall.
spider.contextPassage: A spider builds its web in corners or between trees. Many people are scared when they see a spider.

spoon.definition: A small tool with a round part and a long handle, used to eat or mix food.
spoon.exampleSentence: She used a spoon to eat her soup.
spoon.contextPassage: You use a spoon to eat soft or liquid food. People also use a spoon to stir coffee or tea.

square.definition: A flat shape with four equal sides and four equal corners.
square.exampleSentence: She drew a square on the paper.
square.contextPassage: A square looks like a box shape with all sides the same length. A chessboard is made of small squares.

stage.definition: One part or step in a longer process or activity.
stage.exampleSentence: We are at the first stage of the project.
stage.contextPassage: A stage is one section in something that has many parts. You move to the next stage when the first one is done.

stair.definition: One single step that is part of a set of steps going up or down.
stair.exampleSentence: She slipped on the top stair.
stair.contextPassage: A stair is one of the steps you walk on to go up a building. You go up or down one stair at a time.

stamp.definition: A strong hit of your foot on the ground.
stamp.exampleSentence: He gave a loud stamp with his foot.
stamp.contextPassage: You stamp when you hit the floor hard with your foot. Children sometimes stamp their feet when they are angry.

stay.definition: A period of time you spend in one place.
stay.exampleSentence: We had a short stay at the hotel.
stay.contextPassage: A stay can be a few days or a few weeks in one place. She enjoyed her stay with her grandparents last summer.

steal.definition: When someone takes something that belongs to another person without asking.
steal.exampleSentence: Someone tried to steal my bag at the bus stop.
steal.contextPassage: A steal is the act of taking something that is not yours. It is wrong and can get you in trouble with the police.

step.definition: The movement you make when you lift one foot and put it down in a new place.
step.exampleSentence: She took one small step toward the open door.
step.contextPassage: You take steps when you walk from one place to another. Be careful on wet floors because each step can be slippery.

stomach.definition: The part inside your body that holds food after you eat it.
stomach.exampleSentence: My stomach hurts because I ate too much pizza.
stomach.contextPassage: Your stomach breaks down food after you swallow it. When you are hungry, your stomach can make a loud noise.

stone.definition: A small hard piece of rock that you can find on the ground.
stone.exampleSentence: He picked up a smooth stone near the river.
stone.contextPassage: Stones come in many sizes and colors. People sometimes use stones to build walls or paths in a garden.

store.definition: A place where you can go to buy things.
store.exampleSentence: I went to the store to buy milk and bread.
store.contextPassage: A store keeps many different items for people to buy. You can find food, clothes, or toys in different kinds of stores.

storm.definition: Very bad weather with strong wind, heavy rain, or thunder.
storm.exampleSentence: A big storm hit the town last night.
storm.contextPassage: During a storm, it is safer to stay inside your house. A storm can bring heavy rain and strong winds that break trees.

straight.definition: A part of a road or path that goes forward with no turns or bends.
straight.exampleSentence: The car drove fast along the long straight.
straight.contextPassage: A straight part of the road is easy and safe to drive on. After the straight, the road turned sharply to the left.

strange.definition: Something that is very different from what you know or expect.
strange.exampleSentence: There was a strange smell coming from the kitchen.
strange.contextPassage: When something feels strange, it is unusual or hard to understand. People often look twice at something strange because it surprises them.

stupid.definition: A person who does not think clearly or makes very foolish choices.
stupid.exampleSentence: Don't be a stupid and walk home alone at night.
stupid.contextPassage: Calling someone a stupid is not very kind. It means you think that person made a very silly or careless decision.

such.definition: Something that is very similar to another thing you already know.
such.exampleSentence: I have never seen such a beautiful flower before.
such.contextPassage: You use such to point to something and compare it to other things. It helps you show how big, small, good, or bad something is.

suddenly.definition: When something happens very fast and you did not expect it.
suddenly.exampleSentence: The dog suddenly ran across the busy road.
suddenly.contextPassage: When something happens suddenly, there is no warning before it. People are often surprised because it happens so fast.

suggest.definition: To give someone an idea or option without telling them what to do.
suggest.exampleSentence: My friend suggested a good restaurant near the park.
suggest.contextPassage: When you suggest something, you share an idea for another person to think about. You do not force them to do it.

suggestion.definition: An idea that you share with someone to help them decide something.
suggestion.exampleSentence: She made a suggestion for a new place to eat.
suggestion.contextPassage: You can make a suggestion when someone needs help choosing. A suggestion is not an order, so people can say no.

suit.definition: A set of matching clothes, usually a jacket and trousers or a skirt, worn together.
suit.exampleSentence: He wore a dark blue suit to the job interview.
suit.contextPassage: People often wear a suit to work in an office. You can also wear a suit to a wedding or a formal event.

support.definition: Help or care that you give to someone who needs it.
support.exampleSentence: She gave her friend a lot of support after the bad news.
support.contextPassage: Support can be words, money, or just being there for someone. Friends and family often give support in hard times.

suppose.definition: To think that something is probably true, but you are not completely sure.
suppose.exampleSentence: I suppose the shops will be closed on Sunday.
suppose.contextPassage: You use suppose when you are guessing about something. It shows that you are not one hundred percent sure.

surprise.definition: Something that happens that you did not expect at all.
surprise.exampleSentence: The birthday party was a big surprise for her.
surprise.contextPassage: A surprise can make you feel happy or sometimes worried. People plan surprise parties to make someone feel special.

surprised.definition: To make someone feel shocked or happy because something unexpected happened.
surprised.exampleSentence: The gift surprised her and she started to smile.
surprised.contextPassage: You can be surprised by good or bad news. People often look wide-eyed when something surprises them.

surprising.definition: Making you feel shocked because you did not expect it at all.
surprising.exampleSentence: It was surprising to see snow in April.
surprising.contextPassage: You call something surprising when it is very different from what you expected. Surprising news can be good or bad.

sweet.definition: The pleasant taste that sugar or honey has in your mouth.
sweet.exampleSentence: The cake had a lovely sweet taste.
sweet.contextPassage: Sweet is the opposite of sour or bitter. Fruits like mangoes and strawberries have a sweet taste.

system.definition: A group of things or parts that work together in an organised way.
system.exampleSentence: The school uses a new system to mark student work.
system.contextPassage: A system helps things run in an organised and clear way. Computers, schools, and hospitals all use different systems.

tablet.definition: A small flat device with a screen that you can use like a computer.
tablet.exampleSentence: She used her tablet to watch videos at home.
tablet.contextPassage: A tablet is easy to carry because it is light and flat. Many children use a tablet for school work or games.

task.definition: A piece of work that you need to do or finish.
task.exampleSentence: The teacher gave the students a reading task to finish.
task.contextPassage: A task can be big or small depending on the job. You often have a list of tasks to complete during the day.

taste.definition: The feeling you get on your tongue when you eat or drink something.
taste.exampleSentence: The soup had a strong and spicy taste.
taste.contextPassage: Common tastes are sweet, sour, salty, and bitter. Different foods and drinks have very different tastes.

teaching.definition: An idea or rule that a religion or belief tells people to follow.
teaching.exampleSentence: The teachings of the church say we should help others.
teaching.contextPassage: People often follow the teachings of their religion every day. A leader or holy book usually shares these teachings with others.

technology.definition: Tools, machines, and methods that people use to do things more easily.
technology.exampleSentence: New technology makes it easy to talk to people far away.
technology.contextPassage: We use technology like phones and computers every day. Technology helps us work, learn, and have fun faster.

teenage.definition: Related to a person who is between 13 and 19 years old.
teenage.exampleSentence: My teenage sister loves listening to music on her phone.
teenage.contextPassage: Teenage years are a time when young people grow and change a lot. Many teenage boys and girls enjoy spending time with their friends.

temperature.definition: A number that shows how hot or cold something is.
temperature.exampleSentence: The temperature outside is very low today, so wear a coat.
temperature.contextPassage: You can check the temperature with a thermometer. In summer, the temperature often gets very high.

text.definition: Words that are written on paper or on a screen.
text.exampleSentence: I read the text on the page to find the answer.
text.contextPassage: A book, a sign, or a message can all have text. Text on a phone screen is very easy to read and send.

themselves.definition: Used to talk about the same group of people you already mentioned.
themselves.exampleSentence: The children made the cake themselves without any help.
themselves.contextPassage: We use themselves to show that a group did something alone. For example, the students cleaned the room themselves after class.

thick.definition: The part of a situation that is the most busy or difficult.
thick.exampleSentence: She stayed calm in the thick of the busy market.
thick.contextPassage: We say someone is in the thick of things when everything is very busy. This means they are right in the most active part of something.

thief.definition: A person who takes something that does not belong to them.
thief.exampleSentence: A thief took my bag from the table at the café.
thief.contextPassage: A thief usually takes things without the owner knowing. The police will try to find the thief and get the things back.

thin.definition: Something that has a small distance between its two sides.
thin.exampleSentence: The thin piece of paper tore very easily in my hands.
thin.contextPassage: A thin object does not have much space between its two flat sides. Ice in spring can be very thin and not safe to walk on.

thinking.definition: The action of using your mind to have ideas or solve a problem.
thinking.exampleSentence: Good thinking helped her find the answer to the hard question.
thinking.contextPassage: Thinking carefully before you speak is always a good idea. We also use the word thinking to talk about someone's opinions or ideas.

third.definition: The one that comes after the second in order or position.
third.exampleSentence: She finished in third place in the school running race.
third.contextPassage: Third means number three in a list or order. For example, March is the third month of the year.

thought.definition: An idea or something that comes into your mind when you think.
thought.exampleSentence: I had a good thought about how to fix the problem.
thought.contextPassage: A thought can come to you at any time, even at night. We often share our thoughts with friends to talk about ideas together.

throw.definition: To send something through the air using your hand.
throw.exampleSentence: She will throw the ball to her friend in the park.
throw.contextPassage: You can throw a ball, a stone, or a frisbee. People often throw things when they play sports or games.

tidy.definition: Clean and organised, with everything in the right place.
tidy.exampleSentence: Please tidy your room before dinner tonight.
tidy.contextPassage: A tidy room or desk is easy to work in. The opposite of tidy is messy.

tie.definition: To join two ends of something together with a knot.
tie.exampleSentence: He tied his shoelaces before going to school.
tie.contextPassage: You can tie a rope, a ribbon, or your shoes. You make a knot to keep things together.

tip.definition: The pointed or narrow end at the top of something.
tip.exampleSentence: She touched the tip of her ear gently.
tip.contextPassage: Many things have a tip, like a finger, a pencil, or an ear. The tip is usually the small end of something.

tool.definition: An object you use to help you do a job or task.
tool.exampleSentence: He used a hammer and other tools to fix the shelf.
tool.contextPassage: A tool can be a hammer, a screwdriver, or a saw. Tools make it easier to build or repair things.

top.exampleSentence: There is a lot of snow at the top of the mountain.
top.contextPassage: You can talk about the top of a building, a tree, or a hill. The top is the part that is furthest up.

touch.definition: To put your hand or fingers on something.
touch.exampleSentence: The little girl touched the soft cat very carefully.
touch.contextPassage: You touch things to feel if they are hot, cold, soft, or hard. Please do not touch things in a museum.

tour.definition: A trip where you visit different places in a building or area.
tour.exampleSentence: We went on a tour of the old castle yesterday.
tour.contextPassage: You can take a tour of a city, a museum, or a famous building. A guide often shows you around on a tour.

tourism.definition: The activity of travelling to visit new places for fun or interest.
tourism.exampleSentence: Tourism is very important for this small beach town.
tourism.contextPassage: Many countries earn a lot of money from tourism. People who visit other places for holidays are called tourists.

towards.definition: In the direction of something or someone.
towards.exampleSentence: The dog ran towards the children in the garden.
towards.contextPassage: You move towards something when you go closer to it. She walked towards the door to leave the room.

towel.definition: A soft piece of cloth you use to dry your body or hands.
towel.exampleSentence: She used a big towel to dry herself after the shower.
towel.contextPassage: You find towels in the bathroom or at the beach. A towel is usually made of soft cotton material.

tower.definition: A very tall, narrow building or structure.
tower.exampleSentence: We could see the tall tower from far away in the city.
tower.contextPassage: A tower can be part of a castle or a church. Some towers are very famous, like the Eiffel Tower in Paris.

toy.definition: An object that a child plays with for fun.
toy.exampleSentence: She got a new toy for her birthday.
toy.contextPassage: Children love to play with toys at home or at school. A toy can be a car, a doll, or a ball.

track.definition: A mark or line left on the ground by a person, animal, or vehicle.
track.exampleSentence: We saw animal tracks in the mud near the river.
track.contextPassage: When it snows, you can see tracks in the snow. Animals leave tracks when they walk on soft ground.

tradition.definition: Something that people do every year or on special days because their family has always done it.
tradition.exampleSentence: It is a tradition to eat cake on your birthday.
tradition.contextPassage: Many families have traditions at Christmas or New Year. A tradition is passed from parents to children over many years.

traditional.definition: A person who likes old customs and does not want things to change.
traditional.exampleSentence: My grandfather is a traditional and loves old holidays.
traditional.contextPassage: A traditional person likes to do things the old way. They often follow the same rules and habits as their parents did.

trainer.definition: A person who teaches someone how to do a sport or skill.
trainer.exampleSentence: My trainer taught me how to swim faster.
trainer.contextPassage: A trainer helps you get better at something you want to learn. Many sports teams have a trainer who works with the players every day.

training.definition: To practise a sport or skill to get better at it.
training.exampleSentence: She is training hard for the big race next month.
training.contextPassage: Training helps your body and mind become stronger. Athletes go training every day to prepare for competitions.

transport.definition: The act of moving people or things from one place to another.
transport.exampleSentence: The transport of the boxes took two hours by van.
transport.contextPassage: Buses and trains are a common type of transport in cities. Good transport helps people get to work or school quickly.

traveller.definition: A person who moves from place to place and does not live in one fixed home.
traveller.exampleSentence: The traveller set up a small camp near the road.
traveller.contextPassage: Some travellers move around with their family in caravans. They often stop in different places and do not stay long.

trouble.definition: A difficult or dangerous situation that causes worry or problems.
trouble.exampleSentence: The heavy rain caused a lot of trouble in our town.
trouble.contextPassage: If you are in trouble, it is good to ask someone for help. Trouble can happen at school, at home, or outside.

truck.definition: A large vehicle used to carry heavy things from one place to another.
truck.exampleSentence: The driver parked the big truck outside the shop.
truck.contextPassage: Trucks carry food, furniture, and other heavy goods on the road. You often see trucks on the motorway going to different cities.

twin.definition: One of two children who were born at the same time to the same mother.
twin.exampleSentence: My twin sister and I look exactly the same.
twin.contextPassage: Twins are often very close and do many things together. Some twins look the same, but others can look very different.

typical.definition: Something that is normal or usual for a person, place, or situation.
typical.exampleSentence: A typical day for me starts with breakfast at seven.
typical.contextPassage: A typical weekend for many people includes rest and family time. When something is typical, it happens often and is not a surprise.

underground.definition: A train that travels under the ground in a city.
underground.exampleSentence: I took the underground to get to work faster.
underground.contextPassage: The underground is very busy in big cities. Many people use it every day to travel around.

understanding.definition: To know what something means or how something works.
understanding.exampleSentence: She is understanding the new words in her textbook.
understanding.contextPassage: Understanding a new language takes time and practice. It helps to listen and read every day.

unfortunately.definition: Used to say that something bad or sad happened.
unfortunately.exampleSentence: Unfortunately, we missed the last bus home tonight.
unfortunately.contextPassage: You can use unfortunately at the start of a sentence. It shows that you feel sorry about the news.

unhappy.definition: A person who does not feel happy or good inside.
unhappy.exampleSentence: She was an unhappy child who had no friends at school.
unhappy.contextPassage: An unhappy person often looks sad or quiet. A kind word can sometimes help them feel better.

uniform.definition: A set of clothes that shows you belong to a group or school.
uniform.exampleSentence: All the students wear a blue uniform to school.
uniform.contextPassage: Police officers and nurses often wear a uniform at work. It helps other people know who they are.

unit.definition: A single thing that is part of a larger group or amount.
unit.exampleSentence: We finished the first unit of our English course today.
unit.contextPassage: A unit can be one part of a book or a course. Teachers often use the word unit to talk about one section.

united.definition: To join people or groups together to make one team or group.
united.exampleSentence: The two small teams united to win the big game.
united.contextPassage: When people are united, they work together well. It is easier to solve problems when everyone is united.

unusual.definition: Something that is not normal or does not happen very often.
unusual.exampleSentence: She saw an unusual bird in her garden this morning.
unusual.contextPassage: An unusual thing is different from what you see every day. People often stop and look at something unusual.

upstairs.definition: The upper floor or floors of a building.
upstairs.exampleSentence: The bedrooms are in the upstairs of our house.
upstairs.contextPassage: Many houses have a downstairs and an upstairs. You walk up the stairs to reach the upstairs.

used to.definition: Used to talk about something you did regularly in the past but do not do now.
used to.exampleSentence: I used to walk to school when I was young.
used to.contextPassage: You can use used to with any verb to talk about the past. For example, she used to live in Paris.

user.definition: A person who uses something, like a phone, app, or service.
user.exampleSentence: Every user needs a password to open the app.
user.contextPassage: A user is someone who uses a product or website. Companies want their users to be happy with the service.

usual.definition: The normal or common way that something is or happens.
usual.exampleSentence: She ordered her usual coffee at the café this morning.
usual.contextPassage: When something is the usual, it happens most of the time. You can say as usual to mean the same as always.

valley.definition: A low area of land between hills or mountains, often with a river in it.
valley.exampleSentence: The small village sat quietly in the green valley below.
valley.contextPassage: A valley often has a river running through it. Farmers like to grow food in valleys because the land is flat.

van.definition: A vehicle bigger than a car but smaller than a truck, used to carry things or people.
van.exampleSentence: The driver loaded all the boxes into the white van.
van.contextPassage: People use vans to move furniture or deliver packages. A van has a large empty space inside at the back.

variety.definition: When there are many different types of something in one place or group.
variety.exampleSentence: The market sold a great variety of fresh fruits and vegetables.
variety.contextPassage: Having variety means you have many different choices. A variety of foods helps you eat in a healthy way.

vehicle.definition: Something with an engine that people use to travel or move things from place to place.
vehicle.exampleSentence: A car is the most common vehicle people use every day.
vehicle.contextPassage: Buses, trucks, and motorcycles are all types of vehicles. A vehicle needs fuel to move down the road.

view.definition: What you can see from a place, especially something nice or wide to look at.
view.exampleSentence: We had a beautiful view of the ocean from our hotel room.
view.contextPassage: A good view often makes a place feel special. People like to sit by the window to enjoy the view outside.

virus.definition: A very tiny thing that can get inside your body and make you sick.
virus.exampleSentence: She stayed home because she had a bad virus last week.
virus.contextPassage: A virus can spread from person to person very easily. Washing your hands often helps stop a virus from spreading.

voice.definition: The sound a person makes when they speak or sing.
voice.exampleSentence: She has a very clear and beautiful singing voice.
voice.contextPassage: Your voice can sound loud or quiet depending on how you speak. Teachers often use a loud voice so all students can hear them.

war.definition: A large fight between two or more countries using armies and weapons.
war.exampleSentence: Many people lost their homes during the long war.
war.contextPassage: A war can last for many years and hurt many people. Countries sometimes talk to each other to try to stop a war.

wash.definition: The act of cleaning something with water and usually soap.
wash.exampleSentence: I gave the dirty car a quick wash this morning.
wash.contextPassage: A wash can be done by hand or by a machine. Clothes often need a wash after you wear them outside.

washing.definition: To clean something using water and usually soap.
washing.exampleSentence: She is washing the dishes in the kitchen sink right now.
washing.contextPassage: Washing your hands before eating is very important. Many people spend time washing clothes at home every week.

wave.definition: To move your hand back and forth in the air to say hello or goodbye to someone.
wave.exampleSentence: He stood at the door and waved goodbye to his friend.
wave.contextPassage: People often wave when they see someone they know far away. A wave is a friendly and simple way to say hello.

weak.definition: Not having much strength or power to do hard things.
weak.exampleSentence: She felt very weak after being sick for three days.
weak.contextPassage: A weak person may find it hard to carry heavy things. After a long run, your legs can feel tired and weak.

web.definition: A system of pages and information you can look at on the internet.
web.exampleSentence: She found a good recipe on the web.
web.contextPassage: People use the web every day to find information. You can shop, learn, and watch videos on the web.

wedding.definition: A ceremony where two people get married.
wedding.exampleSentence: My sister had a beautiful wedding last summer.
wedding.contextPassage: People often invite family and friends to a wedding. After the wedding, everyone eats and dances together.

weight.definition: How heavy something or someone is.
weight.exampleSentence: The doctor checked my weight at the hospital.
weight.contextPassage: You can measure your weight on a scale. Some people check their weight every week.

welcome.definition: A warm and friendly greeting when someone arrives.
welcome.exampleSentence: The hotel gave us a very warm welcome.
welcome.contextPassage: A good welcome makes people feel happy and comfortable. The host gave every guest a friendly welcome at the door.

wet.definition: Water or another liquid that makes something not dry.
wet.exampleSentence: There was wet on the floor after the rain.
wet.contextPassage: You should dry your clothes if there is wet on them. A towel is useful for removing wet from your hands.

wheel.definition: A round object that turns and helps things move.
wheel.exampleSentence: The wheel on my bike was broken yesterday.
wheel.contextPassage: Cars and buses need wheels to move on the road. A wheel turns around a central point to make movement easy.

while.definition: A period of time, usually not very long.
while.exampleSentence: Please wait here for a short while.
while.contextPassage: I was gone for a while, but I came back soon. After a while, the bus finally arrived at the stop.

whole.definition: Something that is complete, with no parts missing.
whole.exampleSentence: She ate the whole pizza by herself.
whole.contextPassage: When you look at the whole picture, it makes more sense. He read the whole book in just two days.

whose.definition: A word used to ask which person something belongs to.
whose.exampleSentence: Whose bag is on the table?
whose.contextPassage: You use whose to find out the owner of something. For example, you can ask whose phone is ringing.

wide.definition: In cricket, a ball that goes too far from the player to hit.
wide.exampleSentence: The bowler threw a wide, so the other team got a point.
wide.contextPassage: A wide gives the batting team one extra run. The umpire uses an arm signal to show the players it was a wide.

wild.definition: The natural state of an animal that lives free and is not kept by people.
wild.exampleSentence: The lion lived in the wild all its life.
wild.contextPassage: Animals in the wild find their own food and water. Some animals are hard to see because they live deep in the wild.

wind.exampleSentence: A strong wind blew my hat off today.
wind.contextPassage: Wind can be soft and gentle or very strong. On windy days, people often wear a coat or jacket outside.

winner.definition: A person who wins a game, race, or competition.
winner.exampleSentence: The winner of the race got a gold medal.
winner.contextPassage: You can use 'winner' to talk about sports or games. The winner usually gets a prize or trophy.

wish.definition: A strong feeling that you want something to happen.
wish.exampleSentence: She made a wish before she blew out the candles.
wish.contextPassage: People often talk about wishes on birthdays or special days. You can say 'I wish I had more time.'

wood.definition: The hard material that comes from the inside of trees.
wood.exampleSentence: They used wood to build a small house in the forest.
wood.contextPassage: Wood is a very common building material. People also burn wood to make a fire and stay warm.

wooden.definition: Made from the hard material that comes from trees.
wooden.exampleSentence: She sat on a wooden chair at the kitchen table.
wooden.contextPassage: Many old houses have wooden floors. Wooden furniture like tables and beds is very popular.

working.definition: The way something operates or does its job.
working.exampleSentence: The workings of the old clock were very interesting to see.
working.contextPassage: This word is often used in the plural form. You can talk about the workings of a machine or a system.

worried.definition: Feeling afraid or unhappy because something bad might happen.
worried.exampleSentence: She was worried because her son came home very late.
worried.contextPassage: You can say 'I am worried about my exam tomorrow.' A worried person often cannot sleep well at night.

worry.definition: A feeling of fear or anxiety about something bad that might happen.
worry.exampleSentence: His biggest worry was that he would miss the last train.
worry.contextPassage: Worry is a strong and uncomfortable feeling. You can say 'I have a lot of worries at work right now.'

worse.definition: More bad than something or someone else.
worse.exampleSentence: The weather today is worse than it was yesterday.
worse.contextPassage: You use 'worse' to compare two bad things. For example, a cold is bad, but the flu is worse.

worst.definition: The most bad of all things or people in a group.
worst.exampleSentence: That was the worst meal I ever ate at a restaurant.
worst.contextPassage: You use 'worst' when something is more bad than everything else. You can say 'This is the worst day of my life.'

wow exclam..definition: Something you say when you are very surprised or very impressed.
wow exclam..exampleSentence: Wow, that is a really beautiful dress!
wow exclam..contextPassage: People say 'wow' when they see or hear something amazing. It is a very common word in everyday spoken English.

yet.definition: Used in negative sentences to say something has not happened up to now.
yet.exampleSentence: I have not eaten breakfast yet this morning.
yet.contextPassage: 'Yet' often goes at the end of a sentence. You can ask 'Are you ready yet?' to check if someone is done.

yours.definition: Used to talk about something that belongs to you.
yours.exampleSentence: This blue bag is mine and the red one is yours.
yours.contextPassage: You use 'yours' so you do not have to repeat the noun. For example, you can say 'My phone is old but yours is new.'

zero number.definition: The number 0. It means there is nothing or none of something.
zero number.exampleSentence: The score was zero at the end of the game.
zero number.contextPassage: We use zero to show that there is no amount of something. For example, zero degrees is very cold weather.
